text: BELASCUAIN, a village of Spain, in Navarre, 6 m. SW of Pampeluna, on the Arga river. Pop. 300. There are mineral springs of celebrity here.
BELASORE. See BALASORE.
BELASPUR, a town of Hindostan, on the l. bank of the Sutledge, 180 m. N of Delhi, in N lat. 31° 19'. Pop. 10,000.
BELATA, a village of the Libyan desert, at the E extremity of the oasis of Dakkel.
BELAUGH, a parish in Norfolk, 2 m. SE of Coltishall. Area 810 acres. Pop. 161.
BELAUN, a small island in the Sulu archipelago, to the S of Bassilan, in N lat. 6° 4', E long. 121° 57'.
BELAYE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Luzech, 12 m. W of Cahors. Pop. 1,153.

BELBANK, a township in the p. of Stapleton, Cumberland, 9 m. NE of Brampton. Pop. 124.— Also a township in the p. of Bewcastle, in Cumberland, adjoining the former. Pop. 445. BELBAS, Bolbas, or Bilbossi, a tribe of Kurds in Asiatic Turkey, who derive their name from a village in the sanjak of Sheherzur, about 60 m. NE of that city. These Kurds roam during summer on the steep mountains bordering on the prov. of Ardelan in Persian Kurdistan; during winter they come down, with their numerous families, to encamp in the plain of Arbela, where they live by robbery and pillage. At the approach of spring they retire to their inaccessible mountains, where they preserve their independence. According to Dupre, they can raise 15,000 men. They speak the Kurdish language.

BELBEIS, or Belbeys, a town in Lower Egypt, on the r. bank of the Pelusiac branch of the Nile, 30 m. NNE of Cairo. Pop. 5,000. It was famous in the crusade of the 12th cent. as the first town taken from the Saracens, and held by the invading army as a fortified magazine for supplies. In more modern times it was occupied by the French army, to keep open the communication between Cairo and the coast. D'Anville supposes it to occupy the site of the ancient Pharbætus.

BELBEK, a river of Russia, in the peninsula of and receiving numerous springs and tributaries from the mountain-valleys of Ousenbakh. In its upper course, it is called Kabarta; at Albat it first receives the name of Belbek .- Pallas.
BELBEZE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Sabres. Pop. 1,025.
BELBO, a river of Italy, in the Sardinian prov. of Turin, rising at the foot of the Apennines, and flowing into the Tanaro, to the W of Alessandria, after a course of 50 m.
BELBOR, a town of Austria, in Transylvania, on the frontiers of Moldavia, in the circle of Csik- Szereda.
BELBROUGHTON, a parish in Worcestershire, 6 m. E of Kidderminster. Area 5,350 acres. Pop. 1,765.
BELCAIRE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, arrond. of Limoux .- The cant., comprising 17 com., had a pop. of 8,583 in 1831.- The com. is 20 m. SW of Limoux. Pop. 1,042.

BELCASTRO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Ultra, situated on a rocky eminence on a tributary of the Crocchio, 5 m. above the entrance of that river into the gulf of Squillace, and 15 m. NE of Catanzaro. It is the see of a bishop, and has a cathedral, and a diocesan seminary. Pop. 1,005.
BELCHALWELL, a parish in Dorsetshire, 61 m. WNW of Blandford Forum. Area 950 acres. Pop. 225.
BELCHAMP-OTTO'N, a parish in Essex, 43 m. W of Sudbury. Area 1,600 acres. Pop. 389.
BELCHAMP (St. Paul's), a parish in Essex, 2 m. SE of Clare. Area 2,270 acres. Pop. 731.
BELCHAMP (WALTER), a parish in Essex, 3 m. W of Sudbury. Area 2,110 acres. Pop. 652.
BELCHATOW, a town of Poland, in the circle and 63 m. SE of Kalisch, and 12 m. WSW of Petrikau. It is the smallest town in the kingdom, containing only 30 houses, and one church. Pop. 300.
BELCHENBERG, a mountain of Baden, in the circle of Treisam and Wiesen. It is one of the loftiest summits of the Black forest, rising to the height of 726 toises above sea-level.
BELCHER PORT, an indentation formed by the headland of the same name, on the N coast of Russian America, nearly equidistant from Cape North and Icy Cape, in N lat. 70° 27'.
BELCHER'S ISLANDS, two small groups N and S in Hudson's bay, opposite Richmond gulf, in N lat. 55° 50', W long. 80°.
BELCHERTON, a township of Massachusetts, U. S., in Hampshire co., 11 m. E of Northampton, and 78 W of Boston. Its soil is fertile, and it is well cultivated. Pop. in 1840, 2,554.
BELCHFORD, a parish of Lincolnshire, 5 m. NE of Horncastle. Area 2,390 acres. Pop. 554.

BELCHITE, a town of Spain, in the kingdom of Aragon, in the prov. and 30 m. SSE of Saragossa, on the l. bank of the Almonacid. It is the cap. of a judicial partido, and has a church, an hospital, and several alms-houses. It possesses a few woollen manufactories. Pop. 2,500.
BELCLARE, or CLARE-TUAM, a parish in co. Galway, 3 m. SSW of Tuam. Pop. 2,550.
BELCOE, or Belcoo, a village in co. Ulster, 9 m. WSW of Enniskillen.
BELDEA. See Belley. Mazovia, 13 m. from Leczyca. Pop. 400.

BELEBEI, a town of Russia, cap. of the dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 165 m. NW of Orenburg, and 85 SW of Oufa, on the Belebeika. It is much resorted to by invalids. Pop., consisting chiefly of Cossacks, 700. The district of B. is intersected by a chain of lofty mountains. The principal rivers BEL by which it is watered are the Dioma, Ik or Belebeika, and Ouz.

BELECKE, or Belike, a town of Westphalia, in the circle and 14 m. NE of Arnsberg, and 13 m. SE of Soest, on the l. bank of the Mohne. It has a Benedictine convent, and acidulous springs and baths. Pop. 700. BELED, BELAD, or BILED, an Arabic word signifying 'town,' 'province,' or country;' and of frequent occurrence in the composition of names in Oriental topography.
BELED-EL-DJERID. See BILEDULGERID.

hw: Beled-el-Harem (I. E. 'The Holy Land of Islam')
text: BELED-EL-HAREM (i. e. 'the Holy land of Islam'), a district of Arabia, in the prov. of Hedjaz, stretching along the coast of the Red sea, from the port of Rabagh on the N, to Cape Ibrahim or Ras-Maharem on the S, 201 m. in length, and 84 in average breadth. It is generally mountainous, and is destitute of large streams. The soil is sandy, and extremely sterile. It is regarded with great reverence by the Mahommedans; and in the performance of pilgrimages to its holy cities, Mecca and Jidda, certain ceremonies are observed by them on entering its confines.
BELED-ZOUDA, a small district of Arabia, in the prov. of Yemen, dependent upon Tulla, 57 m. NE of Loheia. It is said to be covered to a great extent with the vine.
BELEE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 85 m. N of Lahore, near the N bank of the Chenaub. —Also a low, flat, swampy island in the Niger, 12 m. SE of Madjie.
BELEF. See BIELEF.

BELEI, or EBELEI, a lake in the Kirghiz steppes, in N lat. 52° 16', E long. 62° 50', 144 m. SE of Troitsk. It is celebrated for the excellent salt which it furnishes to the Kirghiz and the inhabitants of the Russian line terminating to the S of Tobolsk. During the hot season, the salt crystallizes on the borders of the lake; it is then collected in heaps, and taken as need requires. The environs of this lake are impregnated with saline matter, but fresh water occurs in several places, and there are marshy forests. The Kirghiz bathe in this lake during summer, and consider the practice highly beneficial in the cure of several diseases. BELE'M, BELLEM, BETHLEM, or LISBOA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, and comarca of Lisbon, situated on the r. bank of the estuary of the Tagus, 3 m. W of the cap., Lisbon, and contiguous to its suburbs of Alcantara and Junquiera. It contains a royal palace, a richly endowed monastery, in the church of which are entombed many of the sovereigns of Portugal, and an hospital for poor noblemen. In the environs are extensive iron founderies and forges.—B. was founded in 1499, by King Emanuel, and became, after the earthquake of 1755, the residence of the royal family.

BELEM, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, 3 m. NE of Cachoeira.—Also a settlement in the prov. of San Pedro do Rio Grande, on the l. bank of the Uraguay, 6 m. above the confluence of the Arapehi.—Also a settlement in the prov. of Parahiba, in the district of Piauhi.
BELEMCHERU, a small fortress of Hindostan, in the Balaghaut ceded districts, on an eminence 50 m. NE of Guti.
BELEM-DO-PARA. See PARA.
BELEN, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para-

BELDOW, a village of Poland, in the circle of guay, on the r. bank of the Ipane-guazu, 15 m. above the confluence of that river with the Paraguay, and about the same distance SE of Villa-Real-de-la-Concepcion.
BELENA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 18 m. N of Guadalaxara, on an affluent of the Henares.

BELENYES, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Bihar, near the r. bank of the Ujlak, 32 m. SE of Gross-wardein. In the environs are quarries of fine black marble, and mines of iron, copper, and argentiferous lead.

BELESTA, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ariege, and cant. of Lavelanet, on the borders of the forest of Belesta, and near the intermitting spring of Frontestorbe, 21 m. ESE of Foix. It contains iron-forges, and timber, marble, porphyry, and alabaster hydraulic saw-mills, and has an extensive timber-trade. Pop. 2,293.—Also a village in the dep. of Upper Garonne, and cant. of Revel, 7 m. NE of Villefranche. In the environs fine jet is procured.

BELFAST, or SHANKHILL, a parish, partly in the barony of Lower Belfast, but chiefly in that of Upper Belfast, co. Antrim. The town of B., in the old or strict sense of that name, stands in the Upper B. section, but, jointly with the co. Down suburb of Ballymacarett, is exhibited in the census of 1841 as a separate territory. Length, 91 m.; breadth, 5 m.; area, exclusive of the town, 18,263 acres,—of which, 1,148 acres are in Lower B. The census of 1831 does not notice the Lower B. section. Pop. of the whole, exclusive of the town, in 1831, 7,532; in 1841, 10,152. Houses 1,158. Pop. of the Lower B. section in 1841, 239. Houses 43.

BELFAST, a seaport and market-town, a borough, a seat of extensive trade and manufacture, the cap. of Ulster, and the 'Modern Athens' of Ireland, stands at the head of Belfast lough, co. Antrim. It is situated 8 m. SW by S of Carrickfergus; 13 m. SE of Antrim; 46 m. SE by S of Coleraine; 30 m. NE of Armagh; and 80 m. N by E of Dublin. It is the third town of the kingdom in amount of pop.; the second in extent of edificed area, in aggregate value of general trade, and in comparative regularity and beauty; the first in proportionate spirit, though only the second in actual appliances, of literature and science; and incomparably the first in enterprise, intelligence, and general prosperity. About one half of the town stands above, and the other half below, the debouch of the Lagan into Belfast lough. The lowness and flatness of the site, the compactness and regularity of the street alignment, and the very scanty presence of either tower, dome, or other high and large architecture overtopping the aggregate mass of houses, render the exterior aspect of the town, from nearly all vantage-grounds, except such as reveal it in a bird's-eye view, remarkably tame and unpromising; but seen from adjacent high grounds by day, it appears an orderly, tasteful assemblage of uniform streets, neat large houses, pretending mansions, and bulky abodes of manufacture and traffic. The environs of the town, both immediate and more remote, abound in the amenities of landscape. The town, exclusive of Ballymacarett, and of straggling edificed outskirts, is about an Irish mile in length from N to S, and very nearly half an Irish mile in mean breadth. Donegal square is a noble quadrangle of about 200 yards by about 150, at the S end of the town, having the White Linen hall in its centre. The square or quadrangle, in the centre of which stand the buildings of the R. Academical Institution, and the Government School of Design, has a larger area, and a more airy aspect, than Donegal square, although as yet but partially edificed. 'The long bridge of Belfast,' the bridge which carried the thoroughfare across the Lagan into Ballymacarett, and which stood immediately above the commencement of the stream's expansion into Belfast lough, was long regarded as one of the architectural wonders of Ulster. Its arched part extended 840 ft.; and its entire length, including the dead work at the ends, was 2,562 ft. Its arches amounted in number to 21. A new bridge, on the site of the old, was founded in 1841. It consists of 5 semicircular arches, each of 50 feet span, and of a considerable extent of dead work at each end, and has a breadth of 40 ft. between the rangewalls.—Another new bridge, on a site about 670 yards farther up the river, was constructed under the management of a joint-stock company. The Exchange-or the Commercial buildings, as it is here called-was erected by an incorporated company, at the cost of £20,000. It is a large and elegant edifice, with a granite front of Ionic columns, resting on a rusticated basement. The Queen's college, built by means of a parliamentary grant at the expense of £30,000, is a handsome building in the Elizabethan style. It is supported by government, and possesses twenty-one professorships, including Greek, Latin, History and English Literature, modern and Celtic Languages, Mathematics, Natural Philosophy, Chemistry, Logic and Metaphysics, Mineralogy and Geology, Civil Engineering, Agriculture, English law, Jurisprudence and Political Economy, Anatomy and Physiology, Practice of Medicine and Surgery, Midwifery, and Materia Medica. The B. Royal Academical institution, is an extensive range three stories high, presenting rather a good front, but very narrow in proportion to its length. It was erected, in 1810, by means of a public subscription of £30,000; and was, immediately afterwards, incorporated by act of parliament, and endowed by an annual grant for the support of its collegiate department until the latter was transferred to Queen's college. Connected with the institution are a museum and a valuable library. Its affairs are conducted by a president, 4 vice-presidents, 20 managers, and 8 visitors, chosen by the proprietary. The B. academy, a plain and rather large structure, was founded by subscription in 1785. This institution was projected on enlightened principles and a most judicious plan, and formed the model for Belfast college and for many other interesting and valuable academical institutions.—The house of the Natural History and Philosophical Society, is a handsome and convenient edifice, founded in 1830; and contains a valuable and extensive museum.—The Botanical gardens were formed since the year 1830; are situated about a mile from the town; and though small, possess great beauty and interest. The Government School of Design is supported by an annual grant of £500 from the Board of Trade in aid of local subscriptions.—In 1841, there were five newspapers published in the town. B. has not yet figured as a publishing town; yet it produced, in 1704, one of the earliest Irish editions of the Bible, and its printingpresses are numerous, and have produced three or four periodicals, all or chiefly religious, and a profusion of pamphlets, fugitive works, and small volumes. -The Lunatic asylum for the counties of Antrim and Down, stands in an airy and healthful situation about a mile W of the town, and was built by Government at the cost of upwards of £50,000. The Deaf and Dumb, and Blind institution was erected by subscription, in 1841, at a cost of £11,000. It accommodates 100 pupils. There are also two Ragged schools, which feed, clothe, and educate nearly 300 children. B. contains within itself the appliances of producing, from the elementary processes on to completion, the various fabrics of the linen and the cotton manufacture, from the finest cambric to the coarsest canvass. In 1841, the steam power-mills for the spinning of linen yarn had amounted to 25; and a principal one of these employed 800 persons, annu ally consumed 720 tons of flax, and produced yarn to the estimated value of £80,000 a-year. Large factories exist also for weaving shirtings, drills, sheetings, sacking, osnaburghs, and other linen fabrics. But as B. is the great focus of the linen manufacture of Ulster, and has numerous and intricately ramified connections, in the trade of producing and selling linen fabrics, with even distant parts of the province, we must reserve a full view of its linen trade for our article on ULSTER: see that article. The cotton manufacture was at one time flourishing but has grievously declined. There are three cotton-spinning factories in the town and suburbs. The cotton trade of B. is now confined chiefly to the production of muslins or jaconets, twilled cords, unions, a few ginghams, and harness shawls and checked handkerchiefs, by hand-loom labour. The only flourishing branch of this manufacture is the sewed muslin trade, about 40 firms being engaged in it, employing about 150,000 persons in the province. Rope-making was introduced about the middle of the last cent. The manufacture of canvass was established in 1784. Salt was, at one time, an article of extensive manufacture; and soap and candles for exportation. In 1763, including excise, the customs of Belfast amounted to £32,900; in 1784, exclusive of excise, to £101,376; and in 1810, to £425,174 18s. 2½d.; and though, since 1810, they have, with fluctuations, undergone a gradual rise, yet, during several years preceding 1838, they averaged rather less than £400,000, and in 1840, they amounted to £361,502. In 1810, the exports amounted to £2,904,520 19s.; in 1835, to £4,341,794 3s. 7d.; and the imports, same year, £3,695,437 11s. 10d. The chief items in the exports of 1835 were linen (£2,694,000), provisions <math>(£906,597)</math>, feathers, flax, and tow <math>(£186,884)</math>, besides corn, meal, and flour, cotton manufactures, linen yarn and horses; and the most valuable items in the imports were linen yarn (£960,000), woollen manufactures (£480,000), tea, haberdashery and apparel, sugar, coals, culm, and cinders, cotton-wool, tobacco, and corn, malt, meal, and flour.-In 1838 the estimated annual amount of inland carriage to the town was 157,300 tons; and from the town was 207,000 tons.—In 1682, the tonnage of the vessels belonging to the port was 3,307; in 1827, the registered tonnage was 21,557; and, in 1835, it was 32,545. In 1849, the tonnage was 72,000, being nearly equal to the aggregate tonnage of Dublin and Cork.—In 1831, the export tonnage coastwise was 155,418; and, in 1834, it was 174,894. In 1831, the export tonnage to foreign ports was 35,335; and, in 1834, it was 31,665. In 1831, the import tonnage coastwise was 27,947; and, in 1834, it was 30,733. In 1831, the import tonnage from foreign ports was 2,537; and, in 1834, it was 2,395. In 1841, the Report of the Railway Commissioners states that the imports and exports of B. were onefourth of the entire imports and exports of Ireland. The channel, in the upper portion of B. Lough, by which vessels ascended to the quays, and which, from the pool of Garmoyle upwards, had been winding and intricate, and minutely marked by buoys, was, in 1840, straightened and shortened, and rendered more convenient, by a cut or new channel of about a mile in length, executed at a cost, by contract, of £40,000, under the authority of the Ballast Board of B. This improvement was greatly extended, and further important facilities for navigation obtained, by a series of operations on the river and banks, carried out under the direction of the same board, and executed at an expense of £320,000, raised, in virtue of a bill passed by parliament in 1841, by a local loan secured upon the produce of the Tonnage dues. These dues amounted, in 1849, to about £22,000 per annum. The building of ships and other craft, and I steam-vessels, has been carried on with activity during many years in B. A patent slip has been also established. Since 1845, the Town Council have widened many streets, established markets, and in many other ways greatly improved the appearance of the town. The ancient limits of the borough are alleged to have been a circle upon a radius of 3 m. The limits under the Boundary act, 2 and 3 William IV., cut off some small western outskirts of the town, extend from the Mile water on the N, to the Blackstaff river on the S.; and comprehend the whole of the townland of Ballymacarett. The borough was incorporated by charter of 11° James II. The Irish Municipal Bill divided the borough into five wards, called Dock, St. Anne's, Smithfield, Cromac, and St. George's; and appointed each ward to return 2 aldermen, and 16 councillors. B. returns 2 members to parliament: it was, in spite of its size and great importance, a mere pocket-borough of the Marquis of Donegal previous to the Reform; in 1841, its constituency was 4,234.—The census of 1841 includes under the name of B., and exhibits as the component parts of a distinct territory or separate jurisdiction, the three districts of B.-Shankhill within the municipal boundary, B.-Knockbreda or Ballymacarett within the municipal boundary, and B.-Shankhill, without the municipal boundary. All our statistics for 1841, or the great bulk of the whole statistics which are of any value, must in consequence assume the town to consist of these parts. Area of the whole, 1,872 acres; of Belfast-Shankhill within the borough, 966 acres; of Belfast-Knockbreda or Ballymacarett within the borough, 576 acres; of Belfast- Shankhill without the borough, 330 acres. Pop. of the ancient borough as built upon, in 1821, 37,277; in 1831, 53,287. Pop. of the whole town as constituted, in 1841, 75,308; of B.-Shankhill within the borough, 63,750; of B.-Shankhill without the borough, 4,861; of B.-Knockbreda within the borough, 6,697. All the statistics which follow include the whole town. Inhabited houses, 10,906; built uninhabited houses, 1,906; houses in the course of erection, 63. Males, 34,858; females, 40,450; families 15,172. Families residing in first-class houses, 1,360; in second-class houses, 12,716; in third-class houses, 1,077; in fourth-class houses, 19. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 2,532; in manufactures and trade, 9,897; in other pursuits, 2,743. The name Belfast is a corruption of the words Beala-fearsaidh, signifying, in a free translation, 'the town of the ford or ferry at the river's mouth.' The ford in the Lagan, to which the name alludes, was an important pass in the rude and tumultuous history of early times. A castle was naturally erected for controlling the pass; and, though not fixed to any date by existing historical record, is supposed to have been built by the celebrated John de Courcey, or some of his followers. In 1333, the rebellious English murdered William de Burgh, Earl of Ulster, in the castle; and invited the native Irish to their assistance from beyond the Bann, whither they had been driven by the invaders from England. The chiefs of the Irish septs, with their barbarian followers, now poured down upon the northern border of the English Pale, and almost annihilated whatever civilization had been introduced. In 1571, Sir Thomas Smith and his son received from Queen Elizabeth, a grant of B. castle, and of a considerable tract of country around it, and within the districts of Claneboy and Great Ardes. The "Grand Inquisition of the County of Down," which records this grant, states that "in Queen Elizabeth's earldom of Ulster, there be divers parcels of land that be waste, or inhabited by a wicked, barbarous, and uncivil people, some Scottish, and some wild Irish;" and that Sir Thomas Smith and his son, "with a power of Englishmen, agree to subdue all and plant them with faithful subjects." But the younger Smith, who commanded the expedition for enforcing the grant, and who entered Ulster in 1572 with the magnanimous purpose of "subduing all," was defeated and killed; and in consequence, his followers were dispersed, various conditions stipulated in order to the legal establishment of the grant were not fulfilled, and the whole of the lands, along with the castle, reverted to the Crown. In 1604, Sir Arthur Chichester received from James I. a final grant of the castle and circumjacent lands; and, although the town rose to but little importance till a century and a half later, he must be regarded as its founder, and also as the instrumental originator of the general prosperity of the county. In 1708, the castle was destroyed by fire. The celebrated and unfortunate Wentworth, Earl of Stafford, was one of the greatest benefactors of Belfast, and as truly laid the foundation of its manufacturing and commercial importance as Sir Arthur Chichester did that of its town and borough character. During Cromwell's domination, after the Restoration, and up to the Revolution, a period of fifty years, B. was taken and occupied by all the parties who desolated Ireland during that entire period, and was little plundered by either. When the Revolution broke out, the inhabitants hailed the arrival of the Prince of Orange with enthusiasm, and exulted at his accession to the Crown as William III.

BELFAST LOUGH, or CARRICKFERGUS BAY, a large and beautiful marine inlet between the counties of Antrim and Down. It looks out upon the North channel, opposite the Rhinns of Galloway in Scotland; and may be regarded as entering either between Black head in Antrim and Nout head in Down, or between White head in Antrim and Salt- Hill point in Down. The two headlands on the Antrim shore, and also the two on the Down shore, are respectively 23 m. asunder. The width of the exterior entrance is nearly 7½ m.; and of the interior, about 5 m. The distance from White head or from the line between it and Salt-Hill point, to the head of the lough, or the embouchure of the Lagan, is 12 m. The width, opposite Gray's point, 4 m. up from the interior entrace, contracts to a little upwards of 2½ m.; and this width is maintained with little diminution to very near the head of the lough. The chief seats of population on the shore, additional to Belfast, are Carrickfergus, 4 m. up from Whitehead, -Bangor, 2 m. up from Salt-Hill point, -and Hollywood, 6 m. up from Bangor. The lough is so sheltered from prevailing winds by the heights on the W and the NW, as almost always to enjoy a comparative calm. The pool of Garmoyle, 1 m. from Hollywood, and nearly 2 m. from the Antrim shore, affords excellent anchorage; vessels can here ride afloat at low water, even within a cable's length of banks which are then dry.

BELFAST, a seaport of Maine, U.S., in Waldo co., of which it is the cap. It is beautifully situated at the head of Belfast bay, or Penobscot, 30 m. from the Atlantic, and 43 m. E of Augusta. It is intersected by a small river, which is crossed by a good bridge. The harbour is safe and commodious, and admits vessels of the largest dimensions. It is rarely obstructed by ice, and forms consequently the principal mart of the winter-commerce of the Penobscot. B. has an extensive foreign and coasting and fishing trade. Its chief exports are lumber and fish. Its tonnage in 1840 amounted to 38,218. Pop. in 1810, 1,259; in 1820, 2,026; in 1830, 3,077; and in 1840, 4,186.—Also a township of Pennsylvania, U. S., in Bedford co. Pop. in 1840, 720.—Also a township of New York, U. S., in Alleghany co. It presents an undulating surface, and is watered by the Genessee and its tributaries, and is traversed in a SW direction by the Genessee canal. The soil consists generally of sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,646.
BELFAUX, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 3 m. NW of Freyburg, on a tributary of the Saane. Pop. 347.

BELFORD, a parish chiefly in the northern division of the ward of Bamborough, Northumberland, and partly in Islandshire, an insulated portion of the co.-palatine of Durham. It extends 43 m. from E to W; and about 3 m. from N to S; and comprises the market-town of Belford, and the townships of Detchant, Easington, Easington-Grange, Elwick, and Middleton. The soil is fertile; and coal, limestone, and building-stone, are found in abundance. Area 9,380 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,030; in 1841, 1,789. The market-town of B. is 322 m. N by W of London, and 13 m. N by W of Alnwick, on the great post- BEL road between London and Edinburgh, on a gentle eminence within 2 m. of the sea. Pop. 1,500.
BELFORT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, and cant. of Lalbenque. Pop. 1,573.

BELFORT, or BEFORT, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haut- Rhin.—The arrond. comprises an area of 123,106 hectares; and contains 9 cant.: viz., St. Amarin, Belfort, Cernay, Dannemarie, Delle, Fontaine, Giromagny, Massevaux, and Thann. Pop. in 1831, 116,156; in 1836, 121,151; in 1846, 130,236.—The cant. comprises 32 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 15,727. -The town is situated on the l. bank of the Savoureuse, in a valley of the Vosges, at an alt. of 1,110 ft. above sea-level, 30 m. SSW of Colmar, and 26 E of Basle. It is generally ill-built, but is of considerable strength; and contains an ancient castle which, from its commanding position, has given its name to the town. It possesses extensive barracks, a handsome church, a communal college and a library. Pop. in 1789, 4,342; in 1821, 4,612; in 1831, 5,753; in 1836, 5,687; in 1846, 5,425. The principal manufactures are hats, paper, and wax candles. It has also extensive blast furnaces and tanneries. The local position of B., at the point of junction of six great roads, renders it an important entrepot of the trade of Germany and Switzerland; and extensive cattle and sheep fairs are held in it every Monday .- In 1648 B. was ceded by the Austrians to France, and was subsequently fortified by Vauban. In 1659 it was given by Louis to Cardinal Mazarin; and in 1781 it fell into the hands of the duke of Valentinois, by whom it was retained until the Revolution. In 1814 it sustained a siege by the allies.

BELFORT, a castle of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, the cap. of a district of the same name, 15 m. SSE of Coine, and 12 m. SW of Davos. It is situated on a rock; and is of considerable antiquity and strength.

BELFORTE, a town of the duchy of Parma, in the dist. of Borgo-San-Donino, situated at the foot of a hill, near the r. bank of the Taro, 25 m. SW of Parma.—Also a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 9 m. NE of Camerino, near the l. bank of the Chienti.—Also a town in the deleg. and 14 m. W of Urbino on the Foglia.

BELGARD, or BIALOGROD, a town of Prussia, in Pomerania, cap. of the circle of the same name, in the regency and 15 m. SW of Coslin, at the confluence of the Leitznitz with the Pesante. It contains an old castle, three churches, two hospitals; and possesses manufactories of cloth and tobacco. Its commerce consists chiefly in grain, fruit, horses, and cattle. Pop. in 1837, 3,038.—The circle of B. comprises an area of 165 sq. m., and contains a pop. of 20,562. Its principal products are hops and tobacco.

BELGAUM, or BELAGRAMA, a town and fortress of Hindostan, cap. of the taluk or subdivision of the same name in the prov. of Bejapur, and the district of Darwur, 100 m. SW of Bejapur, and 43 m. NW of Darwur, in N lat. 15° 52', and E long. 74° 35'. The town stands on an eminence; and, in 1820, consisted of about 1,309 houses, generally substantially built of the compact ochry gravel which abounds in the locality, and those of the better class spacious and uniform. The fort, which is of great strength and extent, is situated in the plain, and is surrounded by a massive wall faced with stone, and flanked with round bastions of great strength and solidity. Beyond is a broad wet ditch cut to a great depth in the solid rock, and an esplanade 600 yards in length. Its area is of great extent, and contains the ruins of two ancient temples and of several inferior buildings. B. is remarkable for the salubrity of its climate, and the equality of its temperature. Westerly winds prevail during 7 months of the year, and at other periods, E and SE are of most frequent occurrence. The locality possesses also the advantage of an excellent supply of water. In 1818 B. was captured after a long and obstinate resistance by the British. The fort was in a state of complete repair, and contained a garrison of 1,600 men, of whom 70 only fell during the siege. The number of inhabitants, consisting of Maharattas, who compose onethird of the entire pop., Mahommedans, Jains, Brahmins, the Jungum or Lingayut sect, &c., amounted in 1820 to 7,652. The taluk is almost entirely surrounded by the Shahpur pergunnah. It consists to a great extent of gravelly hills covered during the rainy season with rich verdure, but at other periods completely sterile. The prevailing languages of the district are Canarese and Maharatta.
BELGAUTCHY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, and dist. of Rajanahal, on the r. bank of the Issamutty, 45 m. ESE of Nattore.

BELGENTIER, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Var, and cant. of Solliés-Pont, 10 m. NNE of Toulon. It has manufactories of woollen fabrics, paper-mills and tanneries, and an extensive commerce in olives. Pop. 1,322.

BELGERN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony and circle of Torgau, on the l. bank of the Elbe, 40 m. NW of Dresden. It is of great antiquity, and possesses a vote in the provincial diet. It contains a town-house, in front of which is a triumphal column, and an hospital. It possesses a considerable trade in corn, and in the other productions of the locality, which, although mountainous, is generally well-cultivated. The town, from its situation, is liable to destructive inundations. Pop. in 1837, 2,669.

BELGIOJOSO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, cap. of the dist. of the same name, in the prov. and 8 m. ESE of Pavia, and 21 m. S of Milan. It is situated in a fertile plain watered by the Po and Olona, and is generally well built. It is indebted for its principal improvements, inclusive of its magnificent aqueduct, to Galeazzo II. It contains a castle, remarkable for the beauty of its architecture, belonging to the princes of Belgiojoso, in whose possession the town has existed since the 15th century. Pop. 2,800. The dist. contains 29 com., and a pop. of 13,301.
BELGIOLUM. See BAILLEUL.

BELGIRATE, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 4 m. S of Palanza, on the W bank of Lago Maggiore, 28 m. N of Novara. It has a small port, which forms the chief point of communication with the Borromean islands. E by the Dutch portion of the grand-duchy of Luxemburg, and by the Prussian grand-duchy of the Lower Rhine. In the boundary treaty concluded at Vienna on 31st May, 1815, the line comprehending the territories which should compose the kingdom of the Netherlands—formed by the union of Holland and the Belgium provinces—was determined in the following manner:- "It commences from the sea, and extends along the frontiers of France, on the side of the Low Countries, such as they have been rectified and fixed by the 3d article of the treaty of Paris, of 30th May, 1814, as far as to the Meuse, and then along the same frontiers, as far as to the ancient limits of the duchy of Luxemburg: thence it follows the direction of the limits between this duchy, and the ancient bishopric of Liège, till it meets on the south of Deisselt, the western limits of this canton, and of that of Malmedy, to the point where this last touches the limits between the late departments of the Ourthe and the Roer; it runs then along these limits, till they touch those of the late French canton of Eupen, in the duchy of Limburg, and following the western limit of this canton, in the direction of the North, leaving on the right a small part of the late French canton of Aubel, joins to the point of contact of the three aucient departments of the Ourthe, the Lower Meuse, and the Roer. Departing from this point, the said line follows that which separates these two last departments, till when it touches the Worm, a river which empties itself into the Roer, and proceeds along this river till the point where it again meets the limit of these two departments, follows this limit as far as the south of Stellenberg, late department of the Roer, reascends then towards the north, leaving Stellenberg to the right, and cutting the canton of Sittard into two nearly equal parts, in such manner that Sittard and Susteren remain on the left; arrived at the ancient Dutch territory, then leaving this territory on the left, it follows its eastern frontier to the point where it touches the ancient Austrian principality of Gueldres, on the side of Ruremond, and directing itself towards the most eastern point of the Dutch territory to the north of Devalmen continues to embrace this territory. Lastly, parting from the most certain point, it goes to join the other part of the Dutch territory in which Venloo is situated; and it will comprehend that town and its territory. Thence to the ancient frontier of Holland, near Mock, situated below Gennep, it will follow the course of the Meuse, at a distance from the right bank, so that all the places which are not above a thousand German perches, 1,970 of which are equivalent to a fifteenth part of a degree of the meridian, distant from that river shall belong, with their banlieues, to the kingdom of the Netherlands: well understood, however, as to the reciprocity of this principle, that no point of the bank of the Meuse shall make a part of the Prussian territory which cannot approach it within 800 German perches. From the point where the line above described touches the ancient frontier of Holland, up to the Rhine, this frontier shall remain in essential points such as it was in 1795, between Cleves and the United provinces; it shall be examined by the commissioners to be immediately named by the two governments, to proceed to the exact determination of the limits as well of the kingdom of the Netherlands, as of the grand-duchy of Luxemburg, designated in the 4th article, and these commissioners shall regulate, with the aid of experienced persons, every thing relating to the hydrotechnic works, and other points, according to the mutual advantage of the two highcontracting parties, and in the most equitable and suitable manner. This same disposition extends to the determination of the limits in the districts of Kyfwaerd, Lobith, and of the whole territory, as far as Kekerdom. The enclaves Huyssen, Malburg. Lymers, with the town of Sevenaer, and the lordship of Weel, shall make a part of the kingdom of the Netherlands, and his Prussian majesty renounces them in perpetuity for himself and all his descendants and successors." Area and Population.] This territory lies between 49° 31' and 51° 27' N lat., and between 2° 37' and 6° E long. Its greatest length from SE to NW is 173 m.; and its greatest breadth from the most northern part of the prov. of Antwerp, to the southern point of the prov. of Hainault, 112 m. Its form approaches to that of a triangle, the base of which is the French frontier; and its area is computed at 3,252,058 hectares, equal to 8,044,323 English acres, or 12,569 sq. m. The area and pop. of the Belgic provinces, are as follow:- Pop. in 1830. In 1841. It would thus appear that the average pop. of B., in the German sq. m., is 8,089; but that in East Flanders the density of the population is 14,524 or nearly double this; while in Belgian Luxemburg it falls below one-third of the general average. Physical Features.] Belgium is a rich level country, the very paradise of agriculturists. That active industry which will not suffer a weed to grow while it can be eradicated, and turns every little patch of garden or orchard-ground to active profit, is nowhere * Before the cessions. seen to such advantage as here. The farm-houses and cottages of the inhabitants have an air of ease and comfort corresponding with the contented and healthy appearance of their possessors; and the Flemish painters have only copied Nature when they represented the cottages of their fatherland as embosomed among groups of trees and thickets. These artificial beauties, as proofs of industry, comfort, and taste, delight the eye of the traveller, and compensate for the want of those natural features peculiar to other countries. In Hainault, Namur, Liège, and Luxemburg, the hills attain a more considerable elevation, particularly on the E side of the Meuse, where a branch of the Ardennes enters Luxemburg, and penetrates as far as Hainault. The N and W regions are flat and featureless, being in fact a continuation of the rich but monotonous landscape of Holland. In the regions of the S and E, we have a greatly less prolific soil, and extensive tracts of heath or of marsh, and occasionally covered with forests. The coasts are throughout low and sandy, presenting large tracts covered with those sandy undulations called dunes, varying from 1 to 5 m. in breadth, and sometimes attaining an elevation of 20 or even 30 ft. Polders, or alluvial districts which have been reclaimed in part from the surrounding marshes, are numerous on the coast and along the banks of the Scheldt. Forests.] Large and extensive forests exist in some districts of B., particularly in the county of Namur, the southern parts of Liège, in Luxemburg, and along the French frontier. The forest of Mormaut, between the Sambre and Meuse, contains an extent of several leagues. The wood of St. Amand, on the borders of French Flanders, reaches to the neighbourhood of Valenciennes. The forest of Faigne, in Hainault, reaches to Mezieres in Champagne. The forest of Soignes, in the neighbourhood of Brussels, contains above 8,000 acres, which usually yielded an annual revenue of 50,000 florins to the Austrian government. The forests of Murdal, Zaventerloo, Grotenhout, and Turnhout, between Louvain, Brussels, and Vilvorden, are extensive. The forest of Maarlaigne, on the banks of the Meuse, reaches from Namur to the neighbourhood of Philippeville. The wood of Nieppe, near the borders of Arras, is the chief forest in Flanders, reaching as far as the Lys; and there is another near Ypres, stretching to the N, and containing a great number of abbeys and villages. The forest of Poodsberg, on the frontiers of Flanders and Hainault, between Grammont and Lessinis, is a large forest of an orbicular form. These forests chiefly consist of beech, but also contain ber have been in a great measure cut down,--first by the Republicans, who, before the commencement of 1797, had felled no fewer than 1,500,000 trees for the purpose of ship-building; and a greater number were afterwards cut down by Bonaparte's order, to recruit the French navy, and create a new fleet at Antwerp. Rivers. The rivers which intersect this country having their mouths, and a great portion of their course, in Holland, are described at length under the latter head. The Scheldt, on quitting the French territory, crosses part of Hainault, forms on the E the boundary of West Flanders, divides East Flanders into two parts, and forms the line of demarcation between East Flanders and Antwerp. It then divides itself into two principal branches which form the islands composing the province of Zeeland .- This | products of B. The prov. of East and West Flan- BELGIUM. river receives, on its r. bank, the Ruppel, formed by the Nethe and the Dyle, the Dendre and the Haine; on its l. bank, the Durme and the Lys.—The Meuse, -by the Belgians called Mass or Maese,-takes its rise in Lorraine; and 7 leagues from the frontiers of France, at the city of Namur, is joined by the Sambre; after watering the provinces of Namur, Liège, and Limburg, it separates N Brabant from Holland, and, uniting itself to the Waal or Rhine, flows into the German ocean. After this junction a popular error gives the name of Maas or Meuse to the united stream; for it is the Waal, the principal arm of the Rhine, which carries to the ocean the united waters. The Meuse is navigable throughout the whole of its course in B., by boats of from 100 to 150 tons. It is joined on the E by the Semoy, the Chiers, the Lesse, the Ourthe, the Ambleve, the Vesdre, and the Roer; and on the W by the Sambre, the Jaar or Jeer, the Mark, the Dommel, and the Mehaigne. Climate.] The climate of B. is milder, and decidedly more healthy than that of Holland. In a late volume of the proceedings of the Royal Academy of Sciences at Brussels, M. Kickz has communicated the result of above 20 years' observation on the atmospheric constitution of the province of S Brabant. It appears that, on the average, winds from S to W prevailed for 166 days in the year; from W to N for 84 days; from N to E for 92 days; and from E to S for only 23 days. On the average there were in the 365 days, 245 of common wind, 81 of strong wind, 29 of violent wind, and 10 of hurricane. It rained, on the average, 149 days in the 365; viz., 69 days of gentle rain, 48 of showers, 10 of heavy showers, and 22 of tempestuous rain. Soil and Agriculture. The soil of B. is generally a rich sandy loam, sometimes interspersed with fields of clay, but more frequently with large tracts of sand. In the neighbourhood of Ostend, it exhibits considerable heaviness, and seems to partake of the nature of a slimy deposit. The same heaviness of soil will also characterize the polders. The science of agriculture has for ages been successfully cultivated in this country, long esteemed the garden of Europe, although once covered with an uninterrupted forest, only giving place here and there to a barren heath or morass. The following table is taken from the Annuaire de Bruxelles for 1835: Total, in English Hect. of Hect. in Hect. of Provinces. acres of cultivated uncult. roads and cultivated land. land. canals. land. Total, Almost all travellers praise the Flemish farmers for their skill in agriculture, and point out many points in which Flemish husbandry has the superiority over that of England. The excellent cloverwhich here seems indigenous-and the clean crops of flax, barley, and oats, especially attract the attention of tourists. The Flemish farmers never allow the land to lie in fallow. In Brabant the usual way of improving waste or barren land is by sowing it with flax-seed accompanied with clover, which is in such cases much more luxuriant than when sowed with the cerealia, yielding a heavy crop a few months after it is sown. Flax is one of the principal agricultural francs. Betwixt Antwerp and Ghent, a distance of more than 30 m., every third or fourth field is sown with flax, which, by the application of liquid manure and oil-cakes, often produces a return of £50 per are usually from 400 to 600 acres. The industry of the Flemings, without the use of the wheel, plough, or strong English harrow, has within 200 years converted a tract of land, originally a barren and sandy heath, into a rich and beautiful garden; and the proin any part of Great Britain does wheat yield more than from 8 to 10 fold. The land, being generally light, is easily watered, and with the simplest instruments. "After seeing the complex machinery of our better stored homesteads, the stranger is surprised to find the working-tools in B. limited to a spade, scythe, rake, and plough of rude construction. As to drilling, they know nothing about it, consequently a machine for the purpose is unnecessary; and as to such a machine as a clod-crusher, they stand not in need of it, in consequence of the lightness of the land. But the implements they have are quite sufficient for their purpose, as the farms run uncommonly small; and, from the abundant population, manual labour is easily procured. The crops grown are wheat, oats, barley, rye, buck-wheat, and beans; and of roots, potatoes, mangold-wurzel, and turnips; but though both the soil and climate are suited to these last, they come in for very little attention." The total value of the capital employed in agriculture, in both the Belgian and Dutch provinces, has, from apparently accurate tables, been estimated at £433,153,333 sterling, and the gross annual produce of agricultural industry at £50,095,166 sterling; deducting two-thirds of the gross produce to defray the price of seed, manure, labour, repairs of buildings, farming utensils, loss arising from the mortality of cattle, and the cost of the food of men and beasts, the nett yearly produce is £16,698,389. Minerals.] The mines form an important department of national industry in B. There are three mining districts. The first, which comprehends Hainault, contains 150 mines in a superficial extent of 102,415 hectares; the second, which includes Namur and Luxemburg, contains 95 mines in an extent of 30,030 hectares; and the third, which embraces Liege and Limburg, contains 138 mines in an extent of 32,777 hectares. The principal mineral riches consist of coals, of which Hainault produces more than the whole of France. The coal-mines of Mons, Charleroi, Liege, and Marimont, furnish annually 3,200,000,000 kilogrammes. In 1836, 31,190 workmen were employed in 230 coalmines in Belgium, and the products were estimated at 32,000,000 francs. The prov. of Namur turns out annually more coals than the kingdom of France, and B. in general produces half as much again as France. Iron-mines abound in the southern provinces in conjunction with those of coal. There having been but a nominal duty on iron in pigs, the smelters on the Prussian side have long taken all their raw iron from the Belgians, and have only worked their own coal-mines to obtain fuel to forge it: yet the offsets of the Ardennes that run into Prussia are rich in iron ore. The mines in B. are free of control as far as the ordinary arrangements for working them are concerned. In Prussia, government reserves the direction of all mining operations; and the consequence is that, with far greater resources for this description of industry, Prussia produces but twice that quantity. Copper is found in Hainault and Liege; lead in Namur and Luxemburg; zinc in Namur, Hainault, and Liege; and pyrites, calomine, marble, sulphur, and alum, in Liège and Namur. The whole country included between the frontier of France and a line supposed to be drawn from Ostend to Arlon, abounds in marble, slate, stone, and lime. The black marble of Denant is of great value and in high request. Turf is used for fuel to the extent of 12,000,000 of tons annually, valued at £500,000. Not one-half of the Belgian pop. employs coals at present, which are dearer than wood; but with the economy of carriage afforded by railways, and the consequent reduction of price to the consumer, coal will soon become the universal fuel. The only valuable mineral waters are those of Spa, in the eastern quarter of Liege, discovered in the 14th cent. Commerce.] The commerce and manufactures of the Austrian Netherlands were at one period the most flourishing in Europe; but declined rapidly from the moment in which they passed from the mild sway of the house of Burgundy under the iron sceptre of Spain. Other causes, such as foreign competition, and the establishment of political and religious liberty in the United Provinces, contributed to raise Amsterdam upon the ruins of Antwerp. The latter city enjoyed a vast commerce in the 16th cent. In the days of Guicciardino, when in its most palmy state, it contained above 200,000 inhabitants; 2,500 vessels might be seen lying together in the roads; 400 came up with one tide, and it was usual for 500 to go and come in a day; 200 waggons daily arrived with passengers from the neighbouring countries, and 1,000 weekly from Germany, the Hanse towns, Lorraine, and France. Above 10,000 carts were weekly employed in land-carriage; and 500 coaches were maintained by the people of fortune and quality. But the iron sway and bigoted policy of the duke of Alva paralyzed all this commercial activity; and, in 1648, the treaty of Munster, by which the mouth of the Scheldt was in effect shut to the citizens of Antwerp, completed the ruin which Alva had begun. When the French republicans conquered the Netherlands, and incorporated the country with France, the opening of the Scheldt was one of their first cares: a canal was made for joining the Rhine, the Maese, and the Scheldt, and magnificent dockyards constructed, together with a large quay. At the revolution, which separated them from the Dutch, the Belgians lost almost the whole of the trade carried on with the colonies of the etherlands, as these colonies reverted to Holland, to which the large India vessels henceforth proceeded. For about two years after 1830, the external commerce of the country languished; but the reduction of the citadel of Antwerp, and the opening of the navigation of the Scheldt, soon changed the face of affairs. In 1829, the year preceding the revolution, the number of vessels which entered the port of Antwerp was 1,031; the number is now above 1,400 annually; the same proportional increase being observable at the only other seaport, Ostend. Without a single colony, the commerce of Belgium is daily extending. The general trade of B. amounted in the year 1840 to 430,000,000 francs, being about in the proportion of one-fifth compared with that of France,-an extraordinary fact, when it is considered that the pop. of B. is not equal to one-tenth of that of France. Of this amount the imports of B. figured for 246,000,000 francs, and the exports for 184,000,000 francs. The special trade of B. (that is, her own production and consumption) amounted to 346,000,000 francs, of which 206,000,000 were for imports, and 140,000,000 for exports; there remained, therefore, for transit and re-exportation 84,000,000, or 19 per cent. of the general trade. Of the 183,000,000 francs of merchandise exported from B. in 1840, France alone received 85,000,000, or one-half; whilst France only exported into Belgium in the same year 52,000,000 francs of merchandise, or the 20th part of her general exports. Comparing the actual foreign trade of B. in 1840 with that of the year 1832, we have,- Those returns prove that since B. obtained a constitution, the increase in her commercial operations is principally confined to her transit trade and exports. In 1845, the general amount of commercial transactions had risen to 677,000,000 fr., being an increase of 53 millions upon 1844. The import commerce had augmented 19 per cent.; the export commerce 9 per cent. In the import commerce, England holds the first rank, and France the second. The imports of English manufactured goods, however, had diminished by two millions in 1845; while France gained 33 per cent. in the import movement, which was increasing in all articles of commerce, but principally in provisions, especially wines, of which the transmission had much diminished in 1844. The total French imports had risen to 57 millions. The Netherlands and the Zollverein follow France in the the first rank; the Zollverein and the Low Countries next; and England in the fourth rank. France received in 1845, 1083 millions' worth of Belgian mer- 1844; but the imports of the same country increased 24 per cent., and the relative quantities being disproportionate, the balance in favour of England was 79,000,000 francs. It appears, by a recent number of the Moniteur Belge, that since the year 1842 there has been a decrease in the importation of English goods into B., whilst, on the other hand, the exportations to England have been increasing every year since. Compared with the year 1845, and the four preceding years, the value of the goods imported from Great Britain for home-use is 16 and 20 per cent. less. According to the old calculation of commercial value those imports during the year 1846 were of the value of 27,000,000 of francs, and, according to the new calculation of value (as ordered by the royal decree of the 10th of October), of 35,000,000 fr. "The trade with Germany thus balances the traffic with the East and West Indies, which is mainly carried on upon British account. The English manufacturer supplies those countries so cheaply, that he can afford to monopolise them, and sends the returns which are not of a nature to suit the English market to Antwerp, which by this means is recovering its place amongst the trading cities of Europe. Great portion of these colonial wares goes to Germany, a few to France, the rest are scattered about for a market. But it is clear that not only the land-transport, but the railways, are benefitted by this traffic. The old skippers and dealers of Antwerp have also their share; and as this business is managed with foreign capital, it has rendered it possible to lay out so much at home as the railway investments amount to. Antwerp is by this arrangement made a leading station where the goods of all countries are to be had at the cheapest rates, and its business must prosper enormously if nothing be done to prevent its success. The desire to acquire a mercantile navy in all haste has made the Belgians themselves throw difficulties in the way of this traffic. The differential duties imposed on wares imported under a foreign flag are all but sufficient to destroy it. Now, it is clear that if, instead of discouraging this indirect trade, they encouraged it as much as possible, and by making a treaty with England obtained the most favourable footing for their vessels in English ports, it would in time grow so large as to give employment to Belgian vessels as well as others, and would even include many articles of their manufacture. For a country not overburthened with capital to object to trade with the capitals of other countries, shows no great mercantile skill; but we must not forget that laws are nowhere made exclusively by merchants, and least of all in B. Mr. M'Adam forcibly pointed out the hopeless contest which is carrying on in Flanders between the hand-labour of the people and the machinery of England. In 1845, the export of Belgian linen to France, which was 33,000,000 francs in 1838, had declined to 20,000,000 francs in 1845, or about 60 per cent. Here, again, the use of English capital, which would insure a high price for the flax grown, and a market well supplied with cheap yarn for foreigners to purchase in, are rejected, while the internal consumption is heavily taxed, and the people of Flanders are not yet relieved. The duties imposed in B. on the textile fabrics do the manufacturing interests of that country the greatest injury. By the treaty with France, the duties on French linen yarns are not much more than half the duties levied on English yarns. But the French, who cannot spin as cheaply as the English, derive little or no benefit from this concession. It is clear, from the large quantities that pass B. in transit, how advantageous the importation of this half-manufactured article is to those who draw it. The transit of yarn is equivalent to excluding an equal quantity of Belgian piece goods from the market to which the yarn goes." [Correspondent of Daily News.] Rightly to apprehend the industrial position of B., says an excellent article in the Prussian Staats past. B. has been an independent state but a short time. Formerly connected with Spain and Austria, and lastly with Holland, she has had more or less to put up with the advantages and disadvantages of such a connexion. The union with Holland was in every respect conducive to her material interests, for although the latter brought a heavy load of debt into the community, still the Belgians, on their part, manufactured, though at considerable cost, for the rich and ever-improving colonies. The wholesale trade, especially in Antwerp, derived from this source its chief support. But the revolution of 1830 burst asunder the links which united the two countries, and inflicted a severe blow on the trade and industry of both: manufactures stood still, and commerce was a while completely ruined. Throughout the whole country one and the same complaint at the loss of the colonies might be heard; and, with the exception of a few zealous patriots, no one ven- BELGIUM. tured to predict a period of long duration to the newly-acquired independence. It is a proof of the inward strength and rich resources of the country, as well as of the activity of the government, that such a crisis as was brought on by the revolution could be got over. B. was now thrown upon herself alone, had to form a new path, to open new sources, new channels for trade,—a labour which is not to be accomplished in a few years. King William had endeavoured to advance every branch of industry by encouragement and support, and the result had been fortunate, since the colonies for the most part offered a sure market. Nevertheless, by this one-sided, though certain system of export, two important evils were occasioned. In the first place, manufacture moved on at an easy pace, without paying attention to the improvements which had for years been introduced in England, the certainty of the market having caused a want of the spur of competition. In the second place, the wholesale dealers of Antwerp had troubled themselves about no other channels for trade, since that already opened was sufficient. Hence the helplessness of the condition to which B. was brought by her revolution. On the one side was a stagnated industry, which could scarcely stand competition; on the other a trade to which nothing was left but a fine harbour. When we allude to the industry of these times we have especially in view the manufacture of calico cherished by King William, and greatly extended in Ghent. When, after the revolution, labour was again employed in this branch, the factories continued to work in a manner, and according to models, which might do very well for the Indians of Java and Sumatra, but which in other markets, when the manufactures of neighbouring countries were exhibited, must necessarlly appear like shade to light. A crisis was, therefore, inevitable, and in 1835 this crisis was at its height. The manufacturers imagined that the only remedy was to raise the import duty on foreign products, and gained over the Flemish deputies for this measure. The affair was warmly discussed in the chambers, where it was at the same time proposed, for the prevention of smuggling, that all goods should be stamped, and that it should be lawful to search the houses of commercial men for the discovery of smuggled goods. The government firmly resisted these injurious fiscal measures, and taught the manufacturers where the real source of the evil lay; the crisis passed over, manufactures improved, and the export materially increased. Manufactures.] The commercial wealth of B. is mainly composed of four kinds of industry carried on in an extensive form-viz., the extraction of coal, the fabrication of iron,—the spinning of flax, and that of wool. There exist 307 coal-mines, producing 3,500,000 tons of coal, employing 37,000 operatives, and worked by 800 steam engines, equal to the force of 23,000 horses, and realizing an annual value of 40,000,000 francs, of which a third is exported to foreign countries. The woollen manufactures of Verviers, Liège, &c., with a capital of from 80,000,000 to 90,000,000 fr., produce from 20,000,000 to 22,000,000 fr. of cloth, of which three-quarters are exported, and which provide employment for 45,000 operatives. The linen manufacture produces nearly 100,000,000 fr., of which 40,000,000 fr. are exported, and occupies 400,000 individuals. In fine, the iron-works produce annually 50,000,000 fr. worth, of which onethird is exported. Together those manufactures produce a circulation of more than 500,000,000 worth of francs, and occupy 675,000 workmen, or one-sixth of the Belgian population.—The woollen cloths of B. are now preferred to the French; and those of black dye are said to be in colour superior to the English. The principal manufactories are those of Verviers, Liège, Dolhain, Hodemont, Stavelot, Thuin, Poperinghe, and Ypres. In the year 1833, the returns of the Belgian chambers showed that in Verviers alone, 40,000 workmen were employed, the products of their labour amounting to 25,000,000 francs. Stuffs, such as flannels, serges, camlets, &c., are manufactured in all the provinces, but particularly in Antwerp and in Hainault. The manufacture of carpets is likewise considerable. The manufactory of Messrs. Schumacher, Overmann, & Co., at Tournay, is one of the finest in Europe; 1,600 workmen are there constantly occupied. The quantity manufactured annually in this establishment amounts to more than 120,000 metres, 7-8ths of which are for exportation. Other carpet manufactories exist on a smaller scale in Brussels, Antwerp, Ghent, Bruges, and Courtrai.—The linen of Flanders is still held in high esteem, the climate being apparently well-suited for its manufacture; nearly all the cities of the lower provinces manufacture it in abundance; but the productions of the looms of Bruges and Courtrai are considered the most beautiful, and sell for the highest price. There has lately been established at Liège a steam-loom linen factory, in which a 90-horse power engine is employed. In 1836, the returns showed a great increase in the quantity of linen sold in the Belgian markets; the total of the produce of the looms in B. in that year amounted to 750,000 pieces of the value of nearly 100,000,000 of francs. In the manufacturing of flax alone, there are upwards of 400,000 persons employed, or a tenth of the entire population. The manufacture of cotton goods is increasing rapidly, in consequence of the general introduction of the best kinds of machinery, and of steam-power. The cotton manufactures give employment in Antwerp and Flanders to 122,000 workmen, and absorb a capital of 60,000,000 of francs. The total value of the manufactured articles amounted in 1836 to 84,000,000 francs. Since 1836, however, this manufacture may be said to be stationary. In 1833 the weight of raw cotton imported was 6,171,231 kilogrammes of 2 lbs. each; in 1842 the importation was but 6,148,237 kilogrammes. Supposing the protecting duties to secure to the Belgian manufacturer the supply of the home market for coarse goods, he does not yet furnish more than 3 lbs. per head for the consumption of his fellow-citizens annually. - The feeding of silk-worms, and the preparation of silk, is a trade also on the increase. The silk fabrics, now manufactured in this country, are esteemed for their good qualities; and already the exports of these tissues into France exceed the imports from that country. The provinces of Antwerp and Brabant contain the principal silk manufactories. The quantity of native silk produced in 1837 amounted to 1,991 kilogrammes. The lace of B. has been always admired for its texture, and the beauty of the flowered work. Lace of a secondary order is made in abundance in the provinces of Antwerp and Flanders. The fine net gauzes of B. are in great request in foreign countries. Tambour and fine sewing work gives employment to upwards of 50,000 females. Above 2,000,000 of francs' worth of lace and tulles are annually exported.—The mechanical ingenuity of the Belgians is particularly observable in the manufacture of cabinet-work and elegant house-furniture, of which immense quantities are annually exported to England, Germany, and America. In Liège and its environs, including Namur, there cannot be fewer than 20,000 men employed in the iron trade. Machinery is now also fabricated in Brussels, Charleroi, Bruges, Nivelles, Tirlemont, Herné, and Yve. At Charleroi nearly 6,000 workmen are employed in the manufacture of nails.—The porcelain works of B. are now in a thriving condition, and the quality of the articles manufactured rivals those of England, Saxony, and France. In their vases and ornamental designs, the Flemish tendency to indulge in colour and heavy gilding, is perceptible; but their thick heavy ware is of dazzling whiteness. Sugar-refining is carried on upon a very extensive scale, in many parts of the kingdom. The quantity exported from Ghent alone in 1836, amounted to 3,998,320 kilogrammes. The number of breweries amounts to 2,800, and a large portion of their produce is exported. The best beers are made at Lembeck, Brussels, Louvain, Diest, and Hoegarde. Immense quantities of spirits are also annually exported. The manufacture of paper is rapidly improving by the introduction of paper-making machines and English workmen. The books printed at Brussels are now upon as good paper as the greater part of London publications. In the massive designs and rich colouring of their paper-hangings, the Belgians excel most competitors. B., notwithstanding all this amount of industry, is, according to official statements, a territorial mass of pauperism; 25 per cent. of the pop., one milsince, through the agency of devoted ministries, will insist on multiplying religious feast-days for a people perishing for want of daily bread. For the a cruel farce; but for that portion of the lower orders, the more skilled workmen, doubtless, who are in work, and able to procure work, at wages however reduced, this multiplication of holidays condemns them to privations beyond those it is their painful lot to endure from scanty earnings, though in full work. For every working day, thus arbitrarily converted into a feast-day, the unfortunate workman is mulcted in the loss of the one day's wages which he would have had for the day's work. The evil does not rest there. For the sphere of employment is not only narrowed positively but consequentially. The price of commodities is enhanced by the restrictions thus imposed upon the power of production from a given force of machinery and capital. With small profits as the general rate of trade, for products to be so cheap as to meet competition, it is necessary to produce more, so as to economize the cost upon a larger surface of production. But the capital and machinery, which are equal to the six days' work of production weekly, with beneficial results, may remain waste and unproductive of profit by the abridgment of one or more days of the weekly term. Since the separation of these countries, the bank has been reinstituted as a joint-stock bank in 1835, and conducted much after the same plan as formerly; but without any other connexion with the government, than that the director and four administrators are appointed or displaced at the pleasure of the king. It is also compelled to receive such state funds as the minister of finance may think proper to deposit in the bank, and to pay at least 1 per cent. interest for the same. Its cap. is 20,000,000 francs, divided into 20,000 shares of 1,000 f. each. The interest paid is 5 per cent. annually upon the actual sums invested. Any overplus of profit after deducting one-tenth for a reserve fund, forms the dividend paid to the shareholders. This bank is allowed to issue bank-notes of 40 f., 100 f., 500 f., and 1,000 f., to an amount not exceeding the capital, in real value, represented in the coffers of the bank. It is a bank of deposit, circulation, and discount - lends money upon approved securities, purchases bills of exchange, receives in deposit the monies of individuals or of companies, without paying any interest for the same, and has a coffer for the special purposes of a savings'-bank. This bank suspended payment in 1838, having at the time seven millions of notes payable to bearer in circulation, 1,400,000 f. due to depositaires, who lodged their money in its savings'-coffer, 6,000,000 due on ac- BELGIUM. counts current, 2,000,000 due on its obligations for money borrowed, and 20,000,000 in shares due to the bearers of its coupons. This bank-failure, the first which occurred in the kingdom, would have been ruinous to commerce and manufactures, and to the industrious classes generally, if the government had not guaranteed, in the first instance, the repayment of the monies lodged in its coffers as a savings'-bank, and then advanced 2,600,000 f. to enable the bank to take up its notes payable to bearer. By the aid of this timely succour, the bank of B. has resumed its payments, liquidated its obligations, and in 1839 its discounts amounted to 26,000,000, and in 1840 about 36,000,000.—The Commercial bank of Antwerp is a joint-stock bank, established for the purpose of discounting bills of exchange, and lending money on merchandise. Its capital is fixed at £1,000,000 sterling, represented by 25,000 shares of 1,000 f. each." Canals.] The chief of the Flemish canals are those of Bruges and Brussels. - The canal of Bruges leads to Ghent, and is branched out into a number of arms which conduct the traveller to Sluys, Blankenberg, Ostend, Furnes, Nieuport, Dunkirk, Dixmuyde, Menin, and Ypres. In default of a male heir, the king, with the consent of the legislative chambers, may nominate his successor, and in further default of such nomination the throne is declared vacant. The legislative power is vested in the king and two chambers, the senate, and house of representatives. The members of these chambers are elected by citizens paying not less than 20 florins (about 35s.) annually of direct taxes. The number of electors in 1840 was 46,894. The number of deputies is one for 40,000 inhabitants. Each representative must be a Belgian by birth or naturalization. The members of the representative chamber are elected for four years, renewable onehalf every two years. The king has the power to dissolve the chambers either simultaneously or separately. The senate is composed of exactly one-half the number of members in the chamber of representatives, and the senators are elected by the same citizens who elect to that chamber. The senators are elected for eight years. The qualifications requisite for a senator are, that he must be a Belgian by birth or naturalization, domiciled within the kingdom, at least forty years of age, and paying at least 1,000 florins (£84) of direct taxes. The members of the house of representatives are paid for their services at the rate of 200 florins (£16 16s.) monthly, during the continuance of the session. The senators do not receive any pay. The president and vicepresidents of each chamber are nominated by its members at the beginning of each session. The chambers assemble as of right every year on the second Tuesday in November, unless they shall have been previously called together by the king. The session must last at least forty days.
