Population.] The Belges, or Belgians—among whom also the inhabitants of Liège and Luxemburg must be reckoned-speak partly the Flemish, and partly the Walloon language. The former language is chiefly used in Flanders, N Brabant, and a part of S Brabant; the latter in the rest of S Brabant, Hainault, Namur, Liège, and a part of Limburg. French and German are also generally spoken—at least by the higher classes-in most of the towns. The Flemish language has the principal features of the Dutch, with some mixture of French; the Walloon is a corrupted dialect of French. Religion.] While the religion of the United Provinces is Protestantism in the Calvinistic form, both as to doctrine, worship, and government, that of B. is Roman Catholicism. Probably the number of dissenters from this faith throughout B. does not exceed 10,000. Various attempts were indeed made to introduce the principles of the Reformation into this quarter of the Netherlands, but without success. However, in spite of all the endeavours of the Jesuits, the majority of the Flemish Catholics adopted the sentiments of Baius, a doctor of Louvain, and Jansen, bishop of Ypres, respecting predestination and grace. Under the mild sway of the house of Austria, the Belgian Protestants enjoyed toleration. The bishopric of Liège swarms with clergymen; and has been called 'the paradise of priests.' "Religious feeling," says a recent writer, "is in B., as everywhere else, subject to modifications, arising from circumstances, and mainly affected by its incidental connection with politics. There is no tie in B. between the church and the state. The constitution does not formally recognise an established church. Entire liberty of sectarian worship are guaranteed by its 14th, 15th, and 16th articles. The national clergy are paid on the lowest possible scale. The archbishop of Malines has a salary of but £840 a-year. The stipends of the inferior clergy do not average £30." Literature.] The Foreign Quarterly Review gives us the following interesting general literary statistics for Holland and Belgium, in 1830:- "In considering that great highway of civilization, the printing press, we should bear in mind that the larger part of the books printed in the Netherlands are re-impressions of works originally published in other countries, and that consequently the number of books composed and published in the kingdom is not a fair criterion of the avidity with which information is sought after. The number of indigenous publications, exclusive of journals and periodicals, for the three years 1826, 1827, and 1828, was 2,183, and reckoning each work to consist of ten sheets and 600 copies, the result gives an average of 4,633,000 sheets a-year. It was calculated by M. Daru, that France in 1825 had produced 128,000,000 sheets,—an amount about five times greater than this of the Netherlands, having regard to the difference of the population. But as, for the reason we have mentioned, these statements do not give the true relation between the two countries, it should be known that Brussels alone contains 84 presses, and now prints 12,600,000 sheets a-year. being one-tenth of what was printed in all France in 1825, or with reference to the population, one-half, supposing that the Netherlands did not contain a single press out of Brussels. This city, from its situation, seems calculated for a repository for the books of all nations; and the low prices at which foreign works are reprinted here, are an excellent antidote to their being sold too dear at home. The increased activity of the press at Brussels is very remarkable; for in 1815 the number of sheets printed was only 4,050,000; in 1823 it had risen to 8,250,000; and in 1828 it amounted to 12,600,000, having more than trebled in thirteen years. The circulation of newspapers, though regarded by many as tending to divert people's attention from more important studies, is nevertheless too sure a means of diffusing information, to be passed over unnoticed. It may be ascertained precisely by the stamps, which for the Netherlands were, in 1826, 145,739 florins, and for France a sum equal to 165,920 florins. The stamps are the same in both countries, and therefore since France in that year reckoned, according to M. Dupin, 26,420,520 sheets, the Netherlands possessed about 21,900,000, exclusive of literary and scientific journals. In the same year, the newspapers published in England and Wales have been estimated at 25,684,003 sheets; in Scotland, 1,296,549; and in Ireland, 3,473,014. The Netherlands have therefore a circulation of 60,000 sheets of newspapers a-day; France has 72,380, and England, 70,370; which is at the rate of one to every 100 persons in the Netherlands; one to 437 in France, and one to 184 in England." Education.] The same journal to which we are indebted for the above extract, supplies us with the following statistics of education throughout the entire Netherlands:—"Tried by the test of education, the position of the Netherlands is equally favourable as it seems from the amount of its printing. In 1826, out of 3,938 communes there were only 684 without schools, being about the sixth part; while in France two-fifths of the communes are still without schools. In the latter country the children, who frequent the schools, are as 100 to 2,019 inhabitants; in the Netherlands they are as 100 to 947, a proportion exceeded by no country in the world, unless it be Prussia, and one that is the more striking, inasmuch as, comparreligious opinion and protection for the exercise of | ing the number of children between 5 and 15 years old with the population, the utmost that could go to school would be 100 children out of every 521 inhabitants, which some of the best provinces do, at present, nearly reach. We are not near this in England, even if we count by the Sunday schools, whose pupils are to our population as 1 to 11; those of the day schools being as 1 to 21. The ratio in Scotland has been variously stated as 1 to 7, and 1 to 11; and in Ireland both as 1 to 11.5, and 1 to 17, the latter of which is the most probable." Administrative divisions.] B. is divided into 9 provinces, each of which is placed under the local administration of a governor, and a provincial council, elected every 4 years, and the numbers of which are proportioned to the pop. of each prov. The number of these provincial councillors in 1840 was 462, who were chosen by 50,918 electors. The provinces are subdivided into 41 arrondissements, and 2,504 communes. History.] At the fall of the French empire, in 1814, the Belgian provinces-which the battle of Fleurus had annexed to France in 1794—were thrown loose upon Europe without a master, and without any stipulation on the part of the French, whose army simply withdrew from the territory leaving the inhabitants to their fate. Whether, from such an abandonment, the allies acquired any right to dispose of B., as her conquerors, is by no means clear. The Belgians had not, indeed, displayed a zeal for independence equal to that with which the Dutch so spiritedly delivered themselves; but B. was still far from being entirely quiescent at that stirring period, and we find the generals of the allied forces recognising the 'bel elan' of her people towards throwing off the French yoke. The declared object of the Allies, moreover, was to re-establish the independence of the countries oppressed by France. Either, then, the Belgians were entitled to make provision for their own independence, or the rights of Austria to these provinces, as their last legitimate sovereign, revived. M. Dumortier thinks that Austria mistook her interests at this period, in not reconstituting B. into a separate principality, under the government of the archduke Charles, whom the States-general, in 1790 were desirous of having for their hereditary grand-duke. This prince had been very popular while governor-general for a few years previous to the annexation of the B. provinces to France; and in 1831 he was proposed, and supported by a considerable party, as a candidate for the crown. But between 1790 and 1814 the policy of Austria had greatly changed; and she had wisely turned her views to Italy and the south, rather than to the recovery of the 'continental colony,'-as the Abbe de Pradt calls it,—which had cost her so dear in former times, and could not, after so long an union with France, be governed but upon principles which at Vienna would be both new and embarrassing. Austria, therefore, though she at first appointed a governor of the restored provinces, supported him only until the Allies had completed their arrangements, and made no effort to acquire the permanent possession of B. either for herself or any branch of her family. Resolved not to risk her Lombardo-Venetian kingdom,— which indeed she originally received as a consideration for not disturbing France in the possession of the Belgic provinces,— Austria was a willing party to the disposal of B. by the Allies in a new and different way. The Allies, we have said, had engaged to restore their independence to the countries under French dominion, that is, their nationality, their ancient rights and privileges. Accordingly, in Holland, 'Orange boven' was again the order of the day: she returned to her old laws and customs, and to a sovereign of the Orange dynasty. But how fared poor Belgium? Did she recover her ancient liberties, -her charters and privileges, not forgotten during twenty years' union with France, —her much-loved 'joyeuse entree?' No! Her national rights were deliberately sacrificed, and her national feelings violated, in order that she might become an accession of territory to Holland. B. was treated as a conquered country, and delivered over to Holland as a mere appendage,-" Holland representing the person acquiring, and B. simply the thing acquired." The sixth article of the treaty of Paris (30th May, 1814) declared that "Holland, placed under the sovereignty of the house of Orange, shall receive an accession of territory;" and the third of the secret articles annexed to that treaty provided, still more explicitly, that "the establishment of a just equilibrium in Europe requiring that Holland should be constituted in the proportions which may enable her to sustain her independence by her own means, the countries comprised between the sea, the frontiers of France as regulated by the present treaty, and the Meuse, shall be united for ever to Holland." Here we perceive at once, that the object of the allies was the aggrandizement of Holland, with a view to adjust the balance of power in Europe, or, in other words, to strengthen the barriers between France and the Rhine. Neither the people of B. nor those of Holland were in any way consulted, or any pains taken to ascertain their wishes in the matter. The treaty of Paris was carried into effect by the congress of Vienna on the Sth of June, 1815, which constituted and fixed the limits of the new kingdom of the Netherlands. The rejection of the fundamental law proposed for acceptance to the Belgian notables, according to the stipulations of the treaty of London of 21st July, 1814, by a majority of 796 over 527, sufficiently demonstrated the feelings with which this transaction was received by the Belgians. The constitution, however, was forced upon the nation, and was the commencement of that course of injuries and oppressions which in their sixteenth year produced their natural fruit—the Belgian revolution. After the transactions narrated in our historical sketch of Holland, and which terminated in the separation of the Belgian and Dutch provinces, there remained but two ways of deciding the question between Holland and Belgium,—war, or mediation. To the former the people of both countries were sufficiently inclined; but the five great European powers, whose will had given existence to the kingdom of the Netherlands, now interfered, in order, if possible, to settle the affair without further bloodshed. In doing this they had many difficulties to contend with. They were under the necessity of solving some very difficult questions with respect to the obligations under which they had been brought by treaty with the king of Holland. There were also the questions of extent of dominion, and the division of the national debt to be decided. A very early dispute also arose, respecting the possession of the grand-duchy of Luxemburg. To the king of Holland it seemed to belong, from its having come into his possession previous to the union of Holland and B. It had been, moreover, obtained in exchange for the German possessions of the house of Nassau. But the act which erected the kingdom of the Netherlands, brought the duchy of Luxemburg into the same position as the other states: if, therefore, the revolution gave B. a title to be regarded as an independent state, Luxemburg had a right to unite her fortunes in the same cause. Luxemburg, moreover, had been regarded by all parties as a Belgian province, and had united with the Belgians in supplying a portion of the representation. On the 26th of December, 1830, the allied powers recognised the permanent separation of B. and Holland. In February 1831, the Belgian congress proceeded to the election of a king, when the duke of Nemours, son of Louis-Philippe, was elected by 97 votes; 74 having voted for the duke of Leuchtemburg, and 21 for the archduke Charles of Austria. In the name of his son, however, the king of the French refused the crown of B., and the affair was thrown once more into an undecided state: meanwhile the regency was held by Baron Chokier. In April, Prince Leopold of Saxe-Coburg was proposed as king of B.,—an honour which he at first declined; but on matters assuming a better aspect, he allowed himself to be again put in nomination, and, on the 11th of July, was elected by a majority of 56. The courts of Great Britain, Austria, France, Prussia, and Russia, concluded a treaty with King Leopold on the 15th of November, 1831, in which treaty the boundaries of the new kingdom were defined, and its peaceable possession guaranteed to King Leopold. According to this treaty, the Belgian territory is composed of the provinces of South Brabant, Liege, Namur, Hainault, West Flanders, East Flanders, Antwerp, part of Limburg, and part of the grand-duchy of Luxemburg. The history of B. since its last revolution has been very satisfactory. "The trial by jury and the freedom of the press were established on a new footing in the year 1831; the system of jurisdiction was organized in 1832; that of the higher branches of education and of the universities was established by very comprehensive laws and regulations in 1834. A revision of the code had already previously taken place, after which important alterations were made in the penal code; at a later period one of the most remarkable of the Belgian institutions, the provincial and commercial constitution, which is essential to the peculiar political character of the nation, underwent a complete reorganization, after mature consideration; and shortly afterwards the same took place with respect to the army. We may consider," says Professor Arendt, whose summary we quote, "the circle of the great organic laws, from which the new order of things derives its truest sanction, to be completed with few exceptions; and we may with justice expect, from the activity and zeal which animates the legislature, that any chance breaches will be filled up in the next session." The march of improvement since followed by Belgium has amply justified these hopes.

BELGODERE, a canton and commune of the island of Corsica, arrond. of Calvi.—The cant. comprises 6 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 2,979.—The v. is 13 m. E of Calvi. Pop. 752. It was founded in the 11th cent., and commands a fine view of the sea, and of the valley of Fiumeregino, celebrated for its olives.
BELGOROD. See BIELGOROD.
BELGRAD. See KARLSBURG.

BELGRADE, a town and fortress of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Servia, and sanjak of Semendria, at the confluence of the Danube and Save, 4 m. SE of Semlin, 21 m. NW of Semendria, and 540 m. NW of Constantinople; in N lat. 44° 47' 46", E long. 20° 37'. This town,—the most important on the Austrian frontier of the Turkish empire,-consists of three subdivisions; viz., the citadel, -the lower BEL town, -and the suburbs. The citadel occupies a commanding position on the summit of a precipitous rock, which rises in the centre of the town, and is surrounded by a lofty wall, with flanking towers, a triple fosse, and an esplanade 400 paces in breadth. It contains the palace of the pasha of Servia, a fine mosque, and a very deep well. Beyond the wall of the fortress is the Topkhaneh, or manufactory of arms and ammunition. To the W, extending to the Danube, is the Lower town, which is also enclosed by walls and ditches, and contains the palace of the Greek archbishop, 14 mosques, a custom-house, an arsenal, barracks, and an extensive fish-market. The suburbs comprise the Rascian town—so called from its inhabitants-and the Palanka. The former extends along the Save, and is enclosed by walls and palisades; the latter is to the S and E of the fortress. The suburbs form the best built and handsomest portion of B., and contain upwards of 100 churches and mosques, 10 baths, numerous khans, and 2 magnificent palaces, surrounded by gardens. At the mouth of the Save, opposite Rascian town, is Zingaris or Gipsy island; and a little higher up the Danube are three small islands which form a safe harbour. B. has latterly much declined in commercial importance; but it still has considerable manufactures of carpets, silk and cotton fabrics, arms, cutlery, hardware, and leather. The pop., consisting of Turks, Servians, Greeks, Armenians, and Jews, is estimated at 20,000.—This town, called under the Roman domination Singidunum, is of great antiquity. It passed successively under the power of the Visigoths, Goths, and Huns. In 1522 it was taken from Charles V. by the Turkish emperor Soliman. Retaken by the Imperialists, it was again captured by the Turks in 1690, after having been retained by the former two years. In 1717 it fell before the victorious army of Prince Eugene; and by the peace of Passarowitz was secured to the emperor of Germany. It was ceded to the Turks in 1739, but was again held by the Imperialists from 1789 till the peace of 1791. In 1807 it was captured, and in 1813 its citadel and suburbs were destroyed by the Servian insurgents.

BELGRADE, a village in Turkey, in the district of Buyukdéré, where the water for the supply of Constantinople and Pera is collected in a large circular basin, whence it is led off by aqueducts and subterraneous channels.

BELGRADE, a township of Maine, U.S., in Kennebec co., 11 m. N of Augusta. In its environs is a chain of beautiful lakes, which discharge themselves by a stream falling into the Kennebec. Pop. in 1840, 1,748
BELGRADE MILLS, a flourishing village in the preceding township.
BELGRADTCHIK, a fortress of Turkey in Asia, in the prov. of Bulgaria, and in the sanjak and 25 m. SSW of Widin.

BELGRAM, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, 65 m. NW of Lucknow. It is of considerable antiquity, and still presents, in the ruins of fortifications, and of edifices in the best style of Mogul architecture, evidences of its former importance. It is noticed by Abul Fazel in 1582, as being remarkable for the salubrity of its climate, and for the melodiousness of voice possessed by its inhabitants. It was the earliest station of the advanced force of the British in this district of Hindostan.

BELGRANO (PORTO), a harbour at the head of Bahia Blanco, on the coast of Buenos Ayres, in S lat. 38° 40'. The coast is here low, marshy, and covered with saline deposits. The tides rise from 8 ft. to 12 ft.

BELGRAVE, a parish in Leicestershire, 2 m. N of Leicester, intersected by the Soar. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 2,600. BELHAVEL, a lake in the p. of Killarga, in co. Leitrim, 4 m. NW of the head of Lough Allen.
BELHAVEN, a village in the p. of Dunbar, in East Lothian, 1 m. W of Dunbar, and included within the parliamentary boundaries of that burgh.
BELHEIM, a village of the circle of the Rhine, arrond. and 10 m. E of Landau, and 3 m. SW of Germersheim, on the Wirbach. Pop. 1,442.
BELHELVIE, a parish in Aberdeenshire, in the district of Aberdeen, on the coast. Pop. in 1801, 1,428; in 1841, 1,594.
BELIANE, a town of Upper Egypt, in the prov. and 7 m. E of Girjeh, on the l. bank of the Nile. It was formerly of considerable importance, and traces still exist of the great extent of its walls.
BELI BAGH, a village of Anatolia, 8 m. SE of Kankari. In the neighbourhood is a salt mine, at an elevation of 2,500 ft. above sea-level. The salt is remarkably pure.
BELICA. See Belley.

BELICE, or Belici, a river of Sicily, which takes its rise in the district of Palermo, 3 m. S of Parco, runs to the S, crosses the eastern part of the district of Corleone, separates the prov. of Girgenti and Trapani, and after a course of 51 m., falls into the Mediterranean 2 m. E of Marinella, and 8 m. SE of Cas Veterano. It is the ancient Hypsa.
BELICH, or BELEJICH, a small river of Asiatic Turkey, which joins the Euphrates, on the l. bank, near Racca.
BELIDAH. See BLIDAH.
BELIET, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, and cant. of Belin, 2 m. NNE of Belin. It contains some iron-works. Pop. in 1846, 1,088.
BELIKI, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 25 m. SSW of Poltava, in the district and 10 m. NNE of Kobyliaki, on the r. bank of the Vorskla.
BELILOVKA, or BIELOVKA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Kiev, in the district and 17 m. ESE of Makhnova, on a tributary of the Ros.

BELIN, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, arrond. of Bordeaux. The cant. comprises 6 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 7,893. The village is 28 m. SSW of Bordeaux, and has some blast-furnaces and steel-works. Pop. 1,411.
BELINA. See BILIN.

BELINA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Bosnia, sanjak of Trawnik, 20 m. N of Zvornik.—Also a town in the prov. of Servia, in the sanjak and 27 m. WSW of Semendria, on an affluent of the Turia.
BELINDA, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, and 7 m. SE of Futtehpur.
BELIRA. See Balira.
BELISMUM. See BELLEME.

BELITZ, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, in the regency and 12 m. SSW of Potsdam, on the l. bank of the Neiplitz. It consists of about 260 houses, and is surrounded by ancient walls and ditches. Linen is its staple article of manufacture. Flax in considerable quantities is cultivated in the environs. Pop. in 1837, 2,417. BELIZE, BALIZE, or BRITISH HONDURAS, or more properly British Yucatan, a district of Central America, stretching along the SE coast of Yucatan, between the parallels of 15° 53' and 18° 30' N, and the meridians of 88° and 90° W long. The Rio Hondo is the N boundary of the settlement, and separates it from Yucatan. The coast-line between the mouth of this river and that of the Sarstun, in 15° 53', is nearly 200 m. in length. The Sarstun forms the boundary with the state of Honduras. The W boundary, formed by the watershed of the rivers flowing E to the bay of Honduras, and then flowing towards the gulf of Mexico, on the W coast of the peninsula of Yucatan, must nearly coincide with the 90th meridian. Henderson estimates the superficial area at 62,750 sq. m. Coasts and Rivers.] The coasts are lined, at an average distance of 15 m. from the main, with numerous cays, or coral islands, covered with cocoanut trees, and affording a profitable resort to turtlefishers. The largest of these are Ambergrease cay, a narrow islet, 20 m. long, and 3 m. wide, at the N point of the coast, and Turneffe, opposite the town of Belize. A clear navigating space, from 4 to 8 m. wide, lies between these islands and coral reefs, and the main; but the barrier they form, throughout a distance of 130 m., presents only two channels through which vessels of 13 ft. draft can conveniently enter. Abreast of the S part of the settlement, the reef becomes broken, and finally disappears at a distance of 12 m. from the coast of Guatemala, leaving an open navigation to the Golfo Dulce. [Allen.] The coastlands are low and swampy, and during the rainy season are quite inundated from the interior. Running in a line from E to W, between the different rivers on the coast, are beautiful and extensive pineforests. Further inland, the country rises with a sandy soil; while the valleys which run up into the Chama range have a rich soil, and are clothed with magnificent timber.—Between the Rio Hondo and the Sarstun, thirteen other rivers and streams discharge themselves on the coast, in courses nearly parallel to each other. Of these, the largest are the New river, which flows into the same shallow estuary which receives the Rio Hondo; the Belize, which is said to have a NE by E course of 170 m.; and the Sibun. To the S of the Sibun, the hills rise nearer the sea, and the streams have in consequence a shorter course. They are the Manati, Mullins' river, Stand creek, Sitti, Monkey river, Deep river, Golden stream, Rio Grande, Moho river, and Ternash. The Belize has a very tortuous course, and its navigation is impeded by numerous falls. It discharges itself into the bay of Honduras by two mouths, the southern of which divides the town of B. into two parts; the other is 31 m. to the NW. It is navigable for pitpans, or flatbottomed canoes, to within two days' land-carriage of another river which falls into Lake Terminos, a large lagune on the shore of the gulf of Mexico, the W side of which inosculates with the Tabasco river, which is connected with the Guasacualco, so that B. can maintain inland communication with the greater part of Mexico. [Thompson.] Climate.] The climate of B. is considered more healthy than that of most of the Indian islands. The mean annual temp. is 80°. The dry season lasts from March to May inclusive. The rainy season embraces June, July, August, and September. These are the most unhealthy months of the year, disease being engendered by the marsh miasmata. In the latter part of October, and the months of November and December, strong N and NW winds set in, frequently occasioning, if accompanied by rain, a fall of the therm. from about 80° on the previous day, to 60°. The endemic diseases prevalent during the unhealthy period of the year are bilious, remittent, and intermittent fevers; rheumatic affections are also of frequent occurrence. The black pop., both native and African, suffer from pulmonary affections during the colder months when the N winds prevail. On the high grounds towards the river sources, and from 100 to 200 m. inland, the climate becomes more congenial to European constitutions; but habits of sobriety and regularity of living frequently ensure throughout this settlement years of immunity from disease.- [Parl. Report.] Soil and Productions.] On the sides of all the rivers FF F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F intersecting British Honduras, there are belts of land varying in breadth from 100 to 800 yards, formed of a stiff loam of great depth, the product of deposits made by the successive overflowings of the rivers, and covered with magnificent timber; when cleared, these tracts are well-adapted to the cultivation of the sugar-cane, and almost every description of tropical plant. In what are called the Cahoon ridges, the soil, consisting of decayed vegetable matter of a depth of from 2 to 3 ft., cannot be surpassed in fertility; and such tracts are very extensive. Sugar, cotton, coffee, and indigo, might all be extensively raised in this region; only a small quantity of indigo, however, has yet been raised for exportation. Arrowroot and rice are grown to a small extent; and plantains, yams, pumpkins, mandioc, coeoa, maize, and a variety of esculents are grown. The common fruits are the cocoa-nut, orange, mango, shaddock, pineapple, guava, pomegranate, granadilla, tamarind, lemon, papaw, and melon. The date, bread-fruit, and almond tree, have been lately introduced, and thrive well. The finer species of timber are the mahogany, cedar, sapodilla, billywebb, ironwood, rosewood, oak, redwood, and axemaster. The mahogany tree of Honduras (Sivietinia Mahogani) is perhaps the most magnificent of all trees. Such is the enormous size and height of the trunk, and the vast spread of its branches, that the largest oak would dwindle into insignificance in comparison with it. Its wood is not so close-grained and beautiful as that of the Cuba and Hayti mahogany. The grain of the limbs or branches is much closer, and the veins more rich and variegated, than those of the trunk. The largest log ever cut in this country was 17 ft. in length, 57 inches in breadth, and 64 inches in depth. It measured 428 cubic feet, and weighed 15 tons. [Honduras Almanac.] The logwood tree (Hæmaloxylon Capechianum) is also a magnificent tree, but far inferior to the maon account of its colouring matter. The pitch pine, and palmetto, also abound in the forests.-Among the quadrupeds are the red tiger or puma, the black tiger or jaguar, the spotted tiger or leopard tiger, the tiger-cat, the mountain-cow or tapir, the peccary, the agouti, the red and the fawn coloured deer, the antelope, the armadillo, racoon, opossum, fox, gibbonett, monkey, baboon, porcupine, sloth, ant-eater, night-walker, quash, and otter.—Among the birds are the carassow, the guan, various kinds of parrots and parroquets, the partridge, quail, wild duck, Muscovy duck, mallard, snipe, macaw, king-fisher, hawk, crane, flamingo, wild turkey, vulture, toucan, goat-sucker, plover, pigeon, pelican, a variety of doves, woodpeckers, and humming-birds, the black and the brown jay, the mocking bird, banana bird, water-rail, nightraven, trumpeter bird, clucking-hen, stilt bird, and cocorico. [Parl. Report.] Agriculture.] The whole line of coast, from the Rio Hondo to the Sarstun, possesses great agricultural capabilities; but almost no attempt has been made to turn these to advantage; and the practical husbandry of this settlement is still of the most primitive | tons of fustic, 5,565 seroons of cochineal, 101,031 lbs. description. Agricultural implements are limited to the axe, the macheat, and the hoe; the two first are employed in felling trees and lopping branches, the last in clearing the soil when overgrown with weeds. The plough is never used; nor is manure ever applied for improving or preserving the fertility of the soil; but when a piece of land becomes impoverished, it is abandoned for a fresh forest clearance. No at- BELIZE. tempt has yet been made to cultivate the sugar-cane; but the supply of sugar is almost exclusively drawn by the colonists from the town of Bacalar in Yucatan. A little, however, has recently been done towards distilling rum from the cane. Ever since the establishment of the settlement of B. the energies of the inhabitants have been exclusively devoted to cutting mahogany and logwood for the home market; and to the introduction of British manufactured goods for the markets of the Central American republics. The cutting of mahogany is performed by gangs of from 20 to 50 men each, who are located on the different rivers for nine months in the year, and build for themselves a temporary village near the scene of their labour. The wood-cutting commences in February, and continues till May; and all the wood must be drawn to the river down which it is to be floated by the 1st of June. When the river is sufficiently swollen to set in motion the logs which have been tumbled into it, the gangs of workmen embark in canoes, and follow the descent of the mass, to extricate such logs as become entangled by the way. On reaching the sea, they are stopped by a boom, sorted according to the owners' marks, and hauled out of the water into their respective yards to be squared and prepared for shipping. All must be on board the vessels by the 1st of August, so as to enable them to sail on that day. The average expense of mahogany cutting is usually estimated at £100 currency, or £70 sterling, for each labourer per annum, independent of the capital sunk in the purchase of the works, cattle, trucks, gear, craft, tools, &c. Commerce.] The gross results of the trade of British Honduras for the years 1824, 1844, 1845, and 1846, were as follows:— Of the exports in 1846, amounting to £408,188, England received to the extent of £298,771; America. £26,263; the West Indies, £100; and other countries, £7,853. The exports of mahogany for this last-mentioned year exceeded those for 1845 by 2,834,941 superficial feet. The total shipments for 1845 being 9,919,507 feet, compared with 12,754,448 feet in 1846. Of this last-mentioned quantity, 964,627 feet were shipped to the United States, and the remainder to London and Liverpool. There was an increase of 1,212,581 feet shipped "from without the limits of the settlement" over the year preceding. These shipments amounting to 3,186,878 superficial feet of mahogany. Among other articles of export in 1846 were 4,139 ft. of cedar, 4,314 tons of logwood, 19 of sarsaparilla, 529,430 cocoa-nuts, 49 cases preserved turtle, 41 boxes of plants, and 150 barrels of tortoise shell. The total value of exports for 1846 (including the produce received "from without the limits," the property of the resident merchants) shows an increase in 1846 of £91,957 6s. over 1845, whilst that of imports shows a decrease of £36,991 6s. The number of vessels which arrived in the port of Belize for the year ending the 31st of December 1846 was 152, with 39,485 tonnage. Of these 71 were British, with 24,985 tonnage; 39 American, with 4,965 tonnage; 14 West Indian, with 484 tonnage; and 5 foreign, with 436 tonnage. The arrivals "from without the limits of the settlement" at the ports of Truxillo and Omoa during the same period were 23 vessels, with 8,615 tonnage. The imports for 1846 amounted to £213,725, of which England had £151,837, America £54,326, the West Indies £1,305, and other countries £6,256. The chief articles of import from Great Britain were linen, woollen, and cotton goods, hardware, earthenware, wines, spirits, malt liquor, tea, sugar, hams, cheese, butter, preserved meats, soap, shoes, saddlery, gunpowder, shot, ironwork, lime, bricks, paint, oil, slates, hay, stones, anchors, cables, and medicine. From the West Indies were imported turtle, cocoa-nuts, oil, straw-hats, salt, ginger, pimento, and gum. From the United States, the imports were flour, pork, beef, bread, salt fish, coffee, tea, sugar, wine, ale, cider, butter, cheese, candles, lard, potatoes, rice, tobacco, soap, furniture, shingles, axes, bedding, and gunpowder. Population.] The pop. of British Honduras in 1833 was 4,537; in 1845 it amounted to 10,709; of whom 10,410 were coloured, and 399 were whites. The coloured pop. are chiefly Negroes, and of more athletic forms than are seen in any other part of the West Indies. About 1,000 are Caribs, whose fathers were banished in 1795 from the island of St. Vincent, to the island of Ruatan, whence they were invited to locate themselves on the main; and there they have spread from Cape Camaron to the North Stand creek, where they have a large and flourishing village 40 m. to the S of Belize. The whites are extensively occupied in commerce; the Negroes in cutting mahogany and dye-wood, and in fishing. Government.] A superintendent, holding a commission from the governor of Jamaica, to whom he is subordinate, and assisted by an executive council appointed by the same authority, with what is called the Public Meeting, form the legislature of British Honduras. To become a member of the Public Meeting, the qualification is being a British-born subject, and having been three years resident in Honduras, having property to the amount of £300, and being supported by the suffrages of 25 registered voters. A voter's qualification consists in being a British-born subject, having been two years resident in the country, and possessing £60. The revenue and expenditure have been, for the years noted, as follows:- Expenditure. Revenue. The principal item of revenue is a 4s. per gallon duty on wines and spirits, which raised £7,353 in 1846. The next item in fiscal productiveness is a duty of 2s. on tonnage, which raised £3,058 in 1846. Then follow a duty of 1 per cent. on unrated articles, and the fees of the colonial secretary's office. The superintendent's salary is £1,800. The chief justice, who also receives his commission from the governor of Jamaica, has a salary of £1,000. British Honduras is in the diocese of Jamaica. The only church-establishments are in the town of Belize. The Wesleyans and the Baptists have each two chapels in the colony. The Honduras grammar school is conducted on Dr. Bell's system, and attended by about 75 pupils. It is under the control and management of the establishment. The Wes- BELIZE. leyans and the Baptists have each two schools attached to their missions. History.] The Honduras coast was discovered by Columbus in 1502. After the conquest of Jamaica in 1656, the English formed a settlement near Cape Catoche, and on the Laguna de Terminos, on the peninsula of Yucatan, chiefly for the purpose of procuring logwood; and in the treaty of 1670 with Spain, it was stipulated that they should not be disturbed in the exercise of this right. In 1680 these stipulations were violated by the Spaniards, who drove the English settlers from the Laguna de Terminos to the eastern shores of Yucatan, on which they formed a new settlement. The peace of Paris in 1763 guaranteed to British subjects the right to cut, load, and carry away logwood from Honduras. In 1783 another treaty was negotiated, of which the 6th article runs thus: "The intention of the two high contracting parties being to prevent as much as possible all the causes of complaint and misunderstanding heretofore occasioned by the cutting of wood for dyeing, or logwood, and several English settlements having been formed and extended under that pretence upon the Spanish continent, it is expressly agreed that his Britannic Majesty's subjects shall have the right of cutting, loading, and carrying away logwood in the district lying between the rivers Wallis or Belize and Rio Hondo, taking the course of the said two rivers for unalterable boundaries, so as that the navigation of them be common to both nations-to wit, by the river Wallis or Belize from the sea, ascending as far as opposite to a lake or inlet which runs into the land, and forms an isthmus or neck, with another similar inlet, which comes from the side of Rio Nuevo, or New River, so that the line of separation shall pass straight across the said isthmus, and meet another lake formed by the water of Rio Nuevo, or New River, at its current. The said line shall continue with the course of Rio Nuevo, descending as far as opposite to a river, the source of which is marked in the map, between Rio Nuevo and Rio Hondo, and which empties itself into Rio Hondo, which river shall also serve as a common boundary as far as its junction with Rio Hondo, and thence descending by Rio Hondo to the sea, as the whole is marked on the map, which the plenipotentiaries of the two Crowns have thought proper to make use of for ascertaining the points agreed upon, to the end that a good correspondence may reign between the two nations, and that the English workmen. cutters, and labourers may not trespass from an uncertainty of the boundaries." Finally, by articles of convention between Great Britain and Spain, in July 1786, the limits of the English settlement were extended in the manner following: "The English line, beginning from the sea, shall take the centre of the river Sibun or Jabon, and continue up to the source of the said river; thence it shall cross in a straight line the intermediate land, till it intersects the river Wallis, and by the centre of the same river the said line shall descend to the point where it will meet the line already settled and marked out by the commissaries of the two Crowns in 1783, which limits, following the continuation of the said line, shall be observed, as formerly stipulated by the definitive treaty." The third article of this convention proceeds to state that "although no other advantages have hitherto been in question, except that of cutting wood for dyeing, yet his Catholic Majesty, as a greater proof of his disposition to oblige the king of Great Britain, will grant to the English the liberty of cutting all other wood, without even excepting mahogany, as well as gathering all the fruits or produce of the earth, purely natural and uncultivated, which may, besides being carried away in their natural state, become an object of utility or of commerce, whether for food or for manufactures; but it is expressly agreed that this stipulation is never to be used as a pretext for establishing in that country any plantation of sugar, coffee, cocoa, or other like articles; or any fabric or manufacture by means of mills or other machines whatsoever (this restriction, however, does not regard the use of saw mills for cutting or otherwise preparing the wood), since all the lands in question being indisputably acknowledged to belong of right to the Crown who, aided by a small naval force under Captain Moir, gallantly repelled them. Spain having ultimately relinquished all rights of sovereignty in this part of America; and England being in occupation and possession of this tract of country, with the original consent of the aboriginal inhabitants, and after having repelled the attack of 1798, it should seem that the possession of it is now vested in the British crown; though this is disputed by the new republics of Central America, who claim a sovereignty barely exercised by Old Spain. Belize, the cap. of the above territory, is built, as already stated, on both sides of the mouth of the S branch of the river of the same name, on a low flat shore, in N lat. 17° 29', W long. 88° 8'. It consists of a long street running along the shore, and composed of the storehouses and residences of the principal merchants; with several inferior streets diverging from it at right angles. The houses are constructed of wood, and are raised 8 or 10 ft. from the ground on pillars of mahogany. In front of the town there is excellent anchorage for vessels not drawing above 15 ft. At the S extremity of the town are the church, and the superintendent's residence. The barracks are on the N of the town. A spacious iron market-house, a new church, and a powder magazine, have recently been erected. The surrounding country is closed in with woods and marshes to a great depth. Mr. Thompson during a fortnight's stay at B. in the month of July, found the average of temp., night and day, 95°. The greatest part of the import and export trade of Guatemala is carried on by the port of Izabal at the bottom of the Golfo Dolce, and by that of Omoa, on the left of the entrance to that gulf. Small schooners, drawing about 7 ft. water, sail between B. and these ports, making the voyage—a distance of 200 m.—in from 4 to 10 days.—The word Balize or Belize, is a corruption of the Spanish Waliz, a name given to this spot by the Spaniards in consequence, it is said, of its having been the haunt of a buccaneer named Wallace or Wallis. Authorities.] Henderson's Account of the British Settlement of Honduras. 2d. edn., 1811.—Thompson's Visit to Guatemala. Lond. 1829.—Sketch of the Eastern Coast of Central America in Geog. Journ., vol. xi. -Colonel Fancourt's Despatches. -Macgregor's Commercial Tariffs. BELK, Belck, or Bieloi, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Serangani group, 12 m. S of in 56° 26' N lat., and 2° 23' W long.; about 12 m. the island of Mindanao, in N lat. 5° 13', and E long. 125° 15'. It is bold and rocky on the N; and is to a considerable extent well-cultivated. Its principal productions are cocoa-nuts and yellow-wax.

BELKI, or BIELKI, a name generally given by the Russians in Siberia to any range of mountains covered with perpetual snow, but especially to those which detach themselves from the ramifications of the Little Altai, between the Katunaia and the Irtish, and between 50° and 52° N lat., and 82° 20' and 85° 20' E long. These mountains differ considerably in their structure and aspect from all the other mountain-ranges in this region; and nowhere present insulated needles and steep peaks. The Belki are in fact branches of the Snowy mountains called Kholzun, which run towards the W and N. These branches are six in number: the Belki of the Ulba, of the Uba, of the Tighirik, of the Korgon, of the Anui, and of the Bashalak.—The Belki of the Ulba appear between the Turgusson and the Ulba or Gromolowka, in 50° 20' N lat. Their rocks are chiefly composed of serpentine, of jasper, breccia, and porphyry.—To the NW of these are the Belki of the Uba, between the Great and the Little Uba. The geological structure of this range is the same as that of the preceding. It is rich in copper. The mines of Kriukovskoi are the most celebrated. -The Belki of Tighirik extend between the Great Uba and the Inia. To the W they reach to the river from which they derive their name and to the Bielaia; on the E they are separated from the Belki of Korgon by the Kair Kumin, an affluent of the Tsharysh. Their geological structure is the same as that of the two preceding branches. Between the snowy summits of the mountains in this range, broad and steep valleys, with no other vegetation than lichens and dwarf birches, disclose themselves. In summer, elks, stags, reindeer, and rock-goats traverse them; but the most common quadruped is the Lagomys alpinus. Three points of the Belki of Tighirik have been determined barometrically. The highest has an elevation of 940 toises, or 6,024 ft. The mine of beryls has an elevation of 5,896 ft.; and the dwellings of the miners 4,836 ft.—The Belki of Korgon lie to the E of the river of that name. They are also denominated the Belki of Tsharysh, as BEL they are situated along that river which runs NW, and along the Koksun which takes an opposite direction. Their rocks are composed of serpentine, porphyry, and breccia. They wind round the source of the Tsharysh, run to the N, and join the Belki of the Anui, situate to the N of the Tsharysh and to the S of the sources of the Anui. - The mountains of this last range are composed chiefly of granite. Their prolongation to the NW is the most northern of all the Belki which detach themselves from the chain of the Kholzun. This prolongation is granitic, and extends between the Tsharysh, the Maralikha, and the Great and the Little Anui.

BELL, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, in the regency and 17 m. WNW of Coblentz. In the vicinity are extensive lime-quarries. Pop. 554.—Also a township of Pennsylvania, U. S., in Clearfield co. Pop. in 1840, 301.
BELL, a tributary of the Macquarie river, in Australia, which it joins on the l. bank, 1 m. E of Mount Arthur.
BELL BAY, an indentation of the N coast of Clarence island, on the strait of Magalhaens.
BELL POINT, a promontory of Southern Australia, to the E of Fowler's bay, in S lat. 32° 35', and E long. 133° 15′.

BELL-ROCK, a neef in the German ocean, formerly called the Scape, and the Inch cape, situated SE of Arbroath, and 30 m. NW of St. Abb's head; in the direct track of navigation to vessels entering either the frith of Forth or the frith of Tay. The rock is a red sandstone apparently of the same formation with the Redhead in Forfarshire, from which it is 11 m. distant. The reef is altogether about 2,000 ft. in length, of which at spring-tide ebbs a portion of about 427 ft. in length, by 230 ft. in breadth, is uncovered to a height of about 4 ft.; but at high water the whole is covered to the depth of 12 ft. It has a lighthouse upon it. The height of the lantern above high water is 90 ft.; and the lightwhich is revolving, and bright and red alternatelyis seen at the distance of 14 m. in clear weather. The reflectors—which measure 24 inches over the lips-consist of copper coated with silver, and formed into a parabolic curve. The building is of a circular form, measuring 42 ft. diam. at the base, from which it diminishes as it rises, so as to measure only 13 ft. at the top, where the light-room rests. Its height is altogether 115 ft. To the height of 30 ft. it is entirely solid, excepting a drop-hole of 10 inches in diam. for the weight of the machinery which moves the reflectors. The ascent to the door-which is placed at the top of the solid-is by a kind of ropeladder. A narrow passage leads from the door to the staircase, where the walls are 7 feet in thickness; at the top of the staircase, which is 13 feet in height, the walls get thinner, and diminish gradually to the top. Above the staircase, the ascent to the different apartments is by means of wooden ladders; and the remaining 57 feet of masonry is divided by five stone floors into rooms for the light-keepers, and stores for the light. The light-room is 12 ft. diam., and 15 ft. in height; and made chiefly of cast-iron, with a copper roof.
BELL SOUND, an indentation of the SW coast of Spitzbergen, to the N of Bell point, in N lat. 77° 40'.
BELLA (LA), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicato, in the dist. and 15 m. SSW of Melfi, on the SW declivity of M. Caruso. It contains two churches, an hospital, and 3 alms-houses. Pop. 5,800.
BELLA AGUA, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Maranham, in the comarca of Caxias, on a small stream of the same name.
BELLA VILLA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, on the r. bank of the Guapore, in S! lat. 15°.

BELLAC, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne.- The arrond. comprises a superficial area of 181,077 hect. and contains 8 cant., viz., Bellac, Bessines, Chateauponsat, Dorat, Magnac-Laval, Mezieres, Nantiat, and St. Sulpice-les-Feuilles. Pop. in 1831, 80,061; in 1836, 81,457; in 1846, 84,295.—The cant. comprises 6 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 9,853.—The town is situated on the slope of a steep hill, on the r. bank of the Vincou, 1 m. E of the confluence of that river with the Gartempe, and 25 m. NNW of Limoges. Its pop. in 1789 amounted to 2,834; in 1836, to 3,581; and in 1846, to 3,722. It has manufactories of linen, cloth, woollen coverlets, paper, leather, and hats, and a printing establishment. Its chief articles of commerce are timber and chestnuts. A cattle fair is held here monthly.— The name of Bellac is derived from a castle built on the site of the town, by the first count of March, in the 10th century.
BELLACO ISLES, a group of rocky islets in the S Atlantic, off the E coast of Patagonia, to the S of Desvelos bay, in S lat. 48° 40', and W long. 66°.
BELLAGAN, a small fishing village in the p. of Carlingford, co. Louth, 42 m. ESE of Carlingford. Pop. in 1831, 155.
BELLAGHY, a village in the p. of Ballyscullion, co. Londonderry, 4 m. NE by N of Magherafelt. Pop. 739.—Also a village in the p. of Achonry, co. Sligo, 5 m. E of Swineford. Pop. 292.

BELLAGGIO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, the cap. of a dist. of the same name, in the prov. and 24 m. NE of Como, at the extremity of a tongue of land which separates the lakes of Como and Lecco. It is remarkable for the number and beauty of the villas and cottages which adorn its environs. Pliny, the younger, had a country-house in this locality. The dist. comprises 15 com.

BELLA ISOLA, one of the Borromean islands in the Lago Maggiore. It is said to have been but a bare rock of slate, until the middle of the 17th cent., when its proprietor, Count Borromeo, converted it into one of the most perfect specimens of the Italian style of ornamental gardening. Upon the rock, a pyramidal mound, consisting of ten rectangular tiers of terraces supported by arches, has been formed. Groves of oranges and lemon trees grow on these terraces, the stone balustrades of which are decorated with statues; and the whole terminates in a platform surmounted with an equestrian statue.
BELLAIR, a village in the p. of Lemanaghan, King's co., 2 m. WNW of Ballycumber. Pop. in 1831, 81.
BELLAL (EL), a town of Nubia, in the prov. of Sheygya, on the l. bank of the Nile, 10 m. NE of Meroe.

BELLANO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 21 m. NE of Como, situated at the extremity of the valley of Sassina, on the E bank of the lake of Como. It contains several silk-mills, and possesses an active commerce. The environs are adorned with numerous villas; and in the adjacent valley is a magnificent cascade, called L'Orrido di Bellano, formed by the torrent Pioverna, which here forces its way with great noise through a tremendous cleft in the rocks.

BELLANTE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Ultra, in the dist. and 8 m. NE of Teramo. It stands on a height, and contains a parish-church and a public school founded by Ferdinand IV. It has two annual fairs. Pop. 1,496. ~ BEL a mile to the N of the embouchure of the Rubicon, and 21 m. SE of Ravenna.

BELLARY, or Belhari, a judicial subdivision of Hindostan, in the Belaghat ceded districts, in the presidency of Madras. It comprises, besides the territory belonging to its capital, Adoni, Curnul, Guti, Harponelly, Sundur, and Raidrug; but its limits are still undetermined. It is generally mountainous, and is watered by the Vadavati, the Pennair, and the Khundair. On the NW it is separated from Bejapur by the Tungabuddra, and on the N by the Kistna or Krishna, from Hyderabad. The principal production of the soil is cotton. The quantity of this article raised in 1812 was estimated at 2,000 maunds; but from the difficulty of effecting its sale, its cultivation has since greatly diminished.—The total revenue of the district amounted in 1817 to 981,221 pagodas. The pop., from the estimate of government collectors in 1822, was stated at only 927,857. The chief towns are Adoni, Bellary the cap., Bijanagur, Curnul, Guti, and Harponelly.- The town and fort of B. is 265 m. NW of Madras, in N lat. 15° 5', and E long. 76° 59'. It is the headquarters of a civil establishment and military division. Its fort, situated on the summit of a steep, isolated rock, is of a quadrangular form, and is defended by three tiers of fortifications. The pettah, or town-which is also fortified-is of considerable extent; and contains a large and handsome bazaar, adorned with rows of trees, besides barracks for the European troops. The principal cantonments and houses are without the walls, the latter are neatly built, with tiled roofs, and are surrounded with gardens enclosed by little hedges of the milk-plant. BELLARY, BELHARI, or VALAHARI, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, 50 m. NNE of Gurrah. It has suffered greatly from the incursions of the Pindarries, and is now much dilapidated. In the vicinity are some fine Hindu temples.
BELLAS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 8 m. NW of Lisbon, on the river An- Pop. 4,000. There are mineral springs celva. here.
BELLASIZE, a township in the p. of Eastrington, E. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. E of Howden. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 306.
BELLAVISTA, a town of Peru, in the intendancy of Truxillo, in an affluent of the Mayobamba, 30 m. SSE of Mayobamba.
BELLAVISTA (CAPE), a headland on the E coast of the island of Sardinia, in N lat. 40°, 10 m. from Monte Santo, the intermediate coast forming the bay of Tortoli or Ogliastro.

BELLE, a small river of Belgium, on the W side of the prov. of Brabant, formed by the union of several small streams, and flowing W to the Dender, which it joins on the r. bank, 5 m. below Alost.— Also a river of Michigan, U. S., falling into the St. Clair, after a course of 50 m.

BELLE ALLIANCE (LA), a hamlet of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant, on the r. side of the road from Genappe to Brussels, 1 m. NW of the village of Planchenoit, and about the same distance E by S of Hougomont. It was in front of this farm-house that Napoleon in person marshalled his Imperial guards for his last desperate effort at Waterloo.
BELLEAU, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. NW of Alford. Area 650 acres. Pop. 193.

BELLECHASSE, a county of Lower Canada, stretching 19 m. along the St. Lawrence, and watered by the Sud, Boyer, and Bellechasse, all tributaries of the St. Lawrence. Pop. in 1836, 14,965, chiefly French Canadians.
BELLECROIX, a hamlet of France, in the dep.
BELLARIA, a town of the States-of-the-Church, of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Dompierre. Pop. 831.—Also a hamlet and port in the dep. of Morbihan, 10 m. SSE of Vannes.

BELLEEK, or Beleck, a parish and village in co. Fermanagh, on the W side of the lower Lough Erne. Area 12,848 acres. Pop. 2,875.—The v. is at the SW extremity of the p., 4 m. E by S of Ballyshannon. Pop. 251. The Erne forms a fine cataract here.

BELLE FONTAINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Juvigny. Pop. 468 .- Also a com. in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Luzarches. Pop. 244.—Also a com. in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Plombières. Pop. 2,580.—Also a hamlet of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, on the r. bank of the Doubs, 2 m. from Saint-Ursanne.- Also a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, 9 m. NE by N of Montmedy.—Also a village in Logan co., state of Ohio, U. S., 18 m. N of Urbanna.—Also a village in St. Louis co., Missouri, U. S., on the S bank of the Missouri.

BELLEFONTE, a town, the cap. of Centre co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 85 m. NW of Harrisburg, on Spring creek. Pop. 1,032. It is the centre of an extensive iron trade.—Also the cap. of Jackson co., in the state of Alabama, U. S., 166 m. NE of Tuscaloosa. Pop. 400.—Also a village in Pulaski co., Missouri, U. S., 81 m. S of Jefferson.

BELLEGARDE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Creuse, arrond. of Aubusson. The cant. comprises 10 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 11,405. The com. is 7 m. ENE of Aubusson.— Also a com. in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Beaucaire. Pop. 1,541.—Also a com. in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Saint-Galmier. Pop. 1,133.—Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Loiret, arrond. of Montargis. The cant. comprises 12 com. The com., 15 m. W of Montargis, has a pop. of 924.—Also a frontier-town and fortress of France, in the dep. of Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. and 6 m. ESE of Ceret, in the com. of L'Ecluse. It occupies a site 1,440 ft. above sea-level, at the junction of the Valserine with the Rhone; and in the neighbourhood is the celebrated Perte du Rhone, where that river, after rushing through a wild and narrow gorge, plunges under the rocks, and pursues 1,630. a subterranean course for about 120 yards. Near the upper end of this defile is the strong fortress of L'Ecluse, originally planned by Vauban in 1679, but greatly strengthened since 1824 by the French government.
BELLEGARDE (PONT-DE), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Ain, com. of Musinens. Pop. 270.
BELLEGHEM, a commune and town of Belgium, in W Flanders, 3 m. S of Courtrai. Pop. 3,182.

BELLE-ISLE, or Belle-Isle en Mer, a small island of France, in the bay of Biscay, on the S coast of Brittany, between the mouth of the Vilaine and the gulf of Morbihan. Its NW point is in N lat. 47° 23'; and its SE in 47° 16'. It is about 10 m. long, and from 3 to 6 m. broad; and is so surrounded by sharp rocks that there are only three landing-places, and these by no means good; they are the harbours of Le Palais, Sauzon, and Gouldfard, of which the first is exposed, the second shallow, and the third difficult of access. It contains, besides the capital, called Le Palais, three small towns, a number of villages, and in 1831, 8,233 inhabitants. The soil is fruitful in wheat and oats, and the climate so mild that cattle are left abroad during the whole winter. A large quantity of salt is manufactured here from the salt-pits; and the sardine fishery, which forms one of the principal branches of employment, is calculated to yield annually £7,000. It also exports grain and draught horses.—This island in ancient times was called Guedel. It was sold by the Marshal de Retz to the celebrated family of Belle-Isle, a line of the | teaux for several leagues. BEL house of Fouquet, who gave it up to the Crown in 1718, receiving in return the county, afterwards duchy, of Gisors. Before the Revolution it had a separate governor, but now forms, with the adjoining islets of Houat and Hædic, a canton in the dep. of Morbihan, and arrond. of Lorient, comprising the 4 communes of Bangor, Locmaria, Le Palais, and Sauzon. A running sea-fight took place near this island on 20th Nov., 1759, between the French, commanded by Conflans, and the English, under Hawke, in which the former sustained a signal defeat. Belle-Isle was taken two years afterwards by the English, but restored at the peace of Fontainbleau in 1763. This island is 25 m. SW of Vannes, and 28 m. W of Guerande.

BELLE-ISLE (North), an island at the mouth of the straits of that name, between New Britain and the N end of Newfoundland island; in N lat. 52° 0' 33", W long. 55° 11' 20". It is 21 m. in circuit, and lies 16 m. from the nearest land on the coast of Labrador. On the NW side it has a harbour for fishing-vessels, and on the E point a cove which will admit shallops.
BELLE-ISLE (South), an island near the mouth of White bay, on the NE coast of Newfoundland.
BELLE ISLE, a small fertile island on the E coast of Newfoundland, in Conception bay.

BELLE-ISLE-EN-TERRE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, arrond. of Guingamp.—The cant., comprising 6 com., had a pop. of 12,499 in 1831.—The com. is 10 m. W of Guingamp. Pop. 1,079. There are iron-forges here.

BELLE ISLE (STRAIT OF), a channel about 50 m long, and 12 m. broad, which separates Newfoundland from Labrador and the American continent. It is deep, and affords a safe passage to the gulf of St. Lawrence; but is little frequented. There are no harbours on the coast of Newfoundland facing it; and those on the Labrador coast are not considered safe.
BELLELAY, a hamlet of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, bailiwick of Moutier, 3 m. from Tavannes. It is celebrated for its cheeses.
BELLEM, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of E Flanders, arrond. and 15 m. W of Gand. Pop.

BELLEME, or Bellesme, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Orne, arrond. of Mortagne.—The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 15,626.—The com., 10 m. S of Mortagne, has a pop. of 3,413. Cotton-goods are extensively woven here. B. was formerly the cap. of Perche, and a very strong place.
BELLENARES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Etreuil, 10 m. NW of Gannat. Pop. 2,237.

BELLENCOMBRE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Dieppe.—The cant. comprises 15 com., with a pop. of 9,092 in 1831.—The com. is 14 m. SSE of Dieppe, on the Argens. Pop. 927.
BELLENHAUSEN, a town of Hesse-Cassel, near the l. bank of the Lahn, 10 m. S of Marburg.
BELLENTRE, a town of Sardinia, in Savoy, in the Tarentaise, 8 m. ENE of Moutiers, on the r. bank of the Isère.
BELLENZ. See Bellinzone.
BELLERBY, a chapelry in the p. of Spennithorne, N. R. of Yorkshire, 1 m. N of Leyburn. Area 2,540 acres. Pop. 350.
BELLERIVE, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 3 m. from Avenches, on Lake Morat. Pop. 359.
